headline: MoHUA Holds National Interaction with 75 AMRUT Shallow Aquifer Management (SAM) Cities under the ‘Catch the Rain’ Campaign
fullText:
Ministry of Housing & Urban Affairs

MoHUA Holds National Interaction with 75 AMRUT Shallow Aquifer Management (SAM) Cities under the ‘Catch the Rain’ Campaign

Several cities, including Nagpur, Vizianagaram , Bhubaneswar and Korba, highlighted encouraging outcomes under the programme

20 JUL 2026 8:33PM by PIB Delhi

As part of the nationwide ‘Catch the Rain’ campaign, the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA), in collaboration with the National Institute of Urban Affairs (NIUA), organised a national interaction with 75 cities implementing the Shallow Aquifer Management (SAM) programme under AMRUT. The event provided a platform for participating cities to share progress, exchange best practices, and showcase completed groundwater recharge interventions aimed at strengthening urban water security.

The SAM programme supports cities in enhancing groundwater recharge through scientific planning and technical handholding. Under the programme, 75 cities are implementing a range of recharge interventions, including recharge shafts, injection wells, check dams, gabion walls, circular khals and modular recharge pits, contributing to sustainable rainwater harvesting, aquifer replenishment and improved climate resilience.

The interaction was chaired by Smt. D. Thara, Additional Secretary, MoHUA, and moderated by Smt. Isha Kalia, Joint Secretary, MoHUA, with participation from representatives of 75 Urban Local Bodies (ULBs). Cities shared their implementation experiences, key outcomes, challenges and future plans for scaling up groundwater recharge interventions.

Several cities highlighted encouraging outcomes under the programme. Korba announced plans to revive 95 additional defunct borewells following the success of its SAM interventions. Vizianagaram reported improvements in groundwater levels, while Nagpur committed to constructing 100 additional recharge structures during the current monsoon. Bhubaneswar highlighted that SAM interventions have not only enhanced groundwater recharge but have also helped mitigate urban flooding through improved stormwater infiltration.

Groundwater experts emphasised the importance of community participation and local stewardship to ensure the long-term sustainability of groundwater recharge initiatives.

The interaction reaffirmed MoHUA’s commitment to advancing sustainable groundwater management under AMRUT through scientific, nature-based and community-led interventions, while furthering the objectives of the ‘Catch the Rain’ campaign to build water-secure and climate-resilient cities.

(Release ID: 2286822)

headline: Central Bureau of Narcotics (CBN) India thwarts illegal trafficking of 12 crore Tramadol Hydrochloride, a.k.a. “fighter drug” tablets under “Operation Vajra”
fullText:
Ministry of Finance

Central Bureau of Narcotics (CBN) India thwarts illegal trafficking of 12 crore Tramadol Hydrochloride, a.k.a. “fighter drug” tablets under “Operation Vajra”

Entire consignment seized from a warehouse in Delhi; three key suspects arrested from Bengaluru and Kochi

20 JUL 2026 8:31PM by PIB Delhi

The Central Bureau of Narcotics (CBN), under the Department of Revenue (DoR), Ministry of Finance, prevented illegal trafficking and diversion of export consignment comprising 120 million (12 crore) high-strength Tramadol Hydrochloride (250 mg) tablets, weighing approximately 30 metric tons (30,000 kg), arrested three key persons, including one from Bengaluru and two from Kochi.

Preliminary investigation indicated that the consignment, ostensibly destined for Guinea-Bissau, appeared to be intended for diversion to Libya. The entire consignment was seized from a warehouse in Delhi, thereby preventing its diversion into illicit international channels.

The operation, codenamed “Operation Vajra”, was carried out by preventive teams of the Central Bureau of Narcotics, Gwalior, during the period between 10th and 20th July 2026 across Bengaluru, Delhi and Kochi, with effective and efficient coordination from the Directorate of Revenue Intelligence (DRI) Bengaluru, DRI Kochi and others agencies.

Intelligence was gathered that 120 million (12 crore) Tramadol tablets destined for Guinea-Bissau are intended for diversion to Libya. During scrutiny under CBN's robust risk-based export control mechanism, multiple risk indicators were identified, prompting a detailed examination and coordination. Subsequent enquiries undertaken by CBN in coordination with the International Narcotics Control Board (INCB) and the competent authorities of Guinea-Bissau established that the declared destination had been misrepresented, revealing an attempt to facilitate diversion of the consignment.

Based on the findings of the examination, CBN conducted coordinated enforcement action in Bengaluru and arrested the Director of Bengaluru-based Limited Liability Partnership (LLP) firm under the relevant provisions of the NDPS Act, 1985. Further investigation revealed that the principal conspirators were operating from Kerala. Despite repeated attempts to evade law enforcement, sustained intelligence gathering led to the apprehension of the remaining two key conspirators from Kochi. All three accused have been arrested under the NDPS Act, 1985. Further investigation is in progress.

The alert vigilance by CBN India successfully prevented a massive influx of illicit synthetic opioids into active conflict zones. This operation highlights, risk-based profiling and robust intelligence sharing between international law enforcement agencies and cooperation among National agencies to secure global pharmaceutical supply chains.

The successful execution of "Operation Vajra" underscores the effectiveness of intelligence-led enforcement, inter-agency coordination and international cooperation in combating transnational organised drug trafficking and reflects India's robust regulatory framework under the NDPS Act, 1985.

The Central Bureau of Narcotics (CBN) remains committed to safeguarding India's regulatory framework and dismantling transnational drug trafficking networks in furtherance of the Government's vision of Nasha Mukt Bharat and safe use of drugs.

(Release ID: 2286819)

The Prime Minister wrote on X;

"एकाग्रताथ सङ्कल्पः स्नायुवद्‌ वर्द्धनक्षमौ।

headline: Steps Taken to Enhance National Preparedness against Emerging Infectious Diseases
fullText:
Ministry of Health and Family Welfare

Steps Taken to Enhance National Preparedness against Emerging Infectious Diseases

Nationwide VRDL Network under ICMR has been expanded to 163 laboratories, comprising 11 Regional VRDLs, 27 State VRDLs and 125 Medical College VRDLs, to strengthen diagnostic capacity across the country

Infectious Disease Research and Diagnostic Laboratories and four Regional Next-Generation Sequencing Hubs have been established to enhance decentralized diagnostics, genomic characterization of pathogens, standardized data generation and timely reporting

Coordination mechanisms between Central and State health authorities during public health emergencies have been strengthened and tested through simulation exercises and mock drills

21 JUL 2026 1:59PM by PIB Delhi

The Union Health Ministry regularly reviews and strengthens its preparedness for the detection and response to emerging infectious diseases through various national programs and institutions including the Viral Research and Diagnostic Laboratories (VRDL) network, genomic surveillance initiatives and wastewater surveillance for early detection of pathogens of public health importance. The preparedness is further strengthened through participation in simulation exercises, mock drills and rapid outbreak response mechanisms.

The Ministry through the National Centre for Disease Control (NCDC) is implementing Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme (IDSP) which is mandated with surveillance & response to outbreak prone communicable diseases. The nationwide VRDL Network under the Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) has been expanded to 163 laboratories, comprising 11 Regional VRDLs, 27 State VRDLs and 125 Medical College VRDLs, to strengthen diagnostic capacity across the country. In addition, Infectious Disease Research and Diagnostic Laboratories and four Regional Next-Generation Sequencing Hubs have been established to enhance decentralized diagnostics, genomic characterization of pathogens, standardized data generation and timely reporting to support public health surveillance and outbreak response.

Coordination between Central and State health authorities during public health emergencies has been strengthened through real-time disease surveillance and reporting under the Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme (IDSP)/Integrated Health Information Platform (IHIP), regular information sharing, and coordinated outbreak response mechanisms. During outbreaks, the Ministry extends technical support through collaborative outbreak investigations, risk assessment, laboratory confirmation, and deployment of multidisciplinary experts under the National Joint Outbreak Response Team, as required. Coordination mechanisms between Central and State health authorities during public health emergencies have also been tested through simulation exercises and mock drills.

As a part of a comprehensive approach to manage future public health emergencies, Pradhan Mantri - Ayushman Bharat Health Infrastructure Mission (PM-ABHIM) has been launched with the intent to enhance the capacity of primary, secondary and tertiary health care facilities/systems and institutes for identifying and managing any new and emerging diseases.

The Union Minister of State for Health and Family Welfare, Shri Prataprao Jadhav stated this in a written reply in the Rajya Sabha today.

HFW/PQ-Steps taken to Strengthen Infectious Disease Preparedness/21 July 2026/5

(Release ID: 2286953)
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW
The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare is enhancing national preparedness for emerging infectious diseases through strengthened diagnostic networks, genomic surveillance, and improved coordination between central and state health authorities. This initiative includes the expansion of diagnostic laboratories and the implementation of the Pradhan Mantri - Ayushman Bharat Health Infrastructure Mission (PM-ABHIM) to boost healthcare facility capacity.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS
*   **VRDL Network Expansion:** The nationwide Viral Research and Diagnostic Laboratories (VRDL) network under the ICMR has expanded to 163 laboratories, consisting of 11 Regional VRDLs, 27 State VRDLs, and 125 Medical College VRDLs.
*   **Diagnostic Infrastructure:** New establishments include Infectious Disease Research and Diagnostic Laboratories and four Regional Next-Generation Sequencing Hubs to support decentralized diagnostics and genomic characterization.
*   **Surveillance Programs:** The National Centre for Disease Control (NCDC) implements the Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme (IDSP) for outbreak response, supported by real-time reporting via the Integrated Health Information Platform (IHIP).
*   **Preparedness Training:** Coordination mechanisms are tested through simulation exercises, mock drills, and rapid outbreak response mechanisms.
*   **Emergency Response:** During outbreaks, the Ministry provides technical support through the National Joint Outbreak Response Team, which includes collaborative investigations and risk assessments.
*   **PM-ABHIM:** The Pradhan Mantri - Ayushman Bharat Health Infrastructure Mission has been launched to enhance the capacity of primary, secondary, and tertiary healthcare systems to manage new and emerging diseases.

headline: Update on Immunization Programme and Pandemic Preparedness
fullText:
Ministry of Health and Family Welfare

Update on Immunization Programme and Pandemic Preparedness

More than 93% National Immunization Coverage maintained in the last 4 years

Nationwide HPV vaccination campaign launched on 28th February 2026 for approximately 1.2 crore eligible beneficiary girls aged 14 years across all 36 States and Union Territories

ICMR has established a network of more than 160 Virus Research and Diagnostic Laboratories for laboratory-based diagnosis of pathogens

Integrated Training Module for Primary Healthcare Team at Ayushman Arogya Mandir launched in April 2026

21 JUL 2026 1:56PM by PIB Delhi

The details regarding the progress under the Universal Immunization Programme (UIP) and the recent initiatives to introduce new vaccine are given below:

As per the National Health policy 2017 goals, the National Full Immunization Coverage has been maintained more than 90% for the last four years details of which are as follows:

National Full Immunization coverage (FIC)

2022-23 | 2023-24 | 2024-25 | 2025-26

94.7% | 93.7% | 98.3 % | 97.9 %

The Nationwide HPV vaccination campaign was launched on 28th February 2026 for approximately 1.2 crore eligible beneficiary girls aged 14 years across all 36 States and Union Territories. The HPV vaccine is delivered free of cost at designated government health facilities, including Ayushman Arogya Mandirs- Primary Health Centres (PHCs), Community Health Centers (CHCs), Sub-District Hospitals (SDH)/District Hospitals (DHs) & Government Medical Colleges (GMCs). Awareness generation is promoted through IEC materials such as radio jingles, posters, banners, myth-busting digital content and social media platforms to ensure high vaccine uptake among girls particularly in rural, tribal, and urban slum areas.

To prepare against future pandemics/public health emergencies in the country, Union Ministry of Health & Family Welfare has provided the following support to States/UTs: -

Pradhan Mantri - Ayushman Bharat Health Infrastructure Mission (PM-ABHIM) was launched to strengthen public health infrastructure at the primary and secondary levels of care; enhance capacity for disease surveillance, diagnostics, and research for emerging and re-emerging diseases; and develop a resilient health system capable of responding effectively to public health emergencies.

Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme (IDSP) was strengthened for carrying out disease surveillance activities. IDSP allows for a decentralized system of response through trained multi-disciplinary Rapid Response Teams (RRTs).

Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) has established a network of more than 160 Virus Research and Diagnostic Laboratories (VRDLs) for laboratory-based diagnosis of pathogens.

Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, launched the Integrated Training Module for the Primary Healthcare Team at Ayushman Arogya Mandir in April 2026. The Integrated Training is a single, structured, and competency-driven framework for Medical Officers (MO), Staff Nurses (SN), Community Health Officers (CHO), Auxiliary Nurse Midwives (ANM) and ASHAs (and ASHA Facilitators).

The following steps were taken to boost medical oxygen infrastructure in the States/UTs: -

Oxygen cylinders, oxygen concentrators & Liquid Medical Oxygen tanks were supplied to the States/UTs.

Pressure Swing Adsorption plants (PSA) plants and Medical Gas Pipeline Systems were established in States/UTs.

Integrated Health Information Platform (IHIP), Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme (IDSP), Health Management Information System (HMIS), Ayushman Arogya Mandir (AAM) Portal and National Program for Non-Communicable Disease (NP-NCD) Portal are presently operational. These portals enable real-time data collection, data analysis, data visualization and dissemination of epidemiological information to support early detection of outbreaks.

The Union Minister of State for Health and Family Welfare, Smt. Anupriya Patel stated this in a written reply in the Rajya Sabha today.

HFW/PQ-Update on Immunization Programme and Pandemic Preparedness/21 July 2026/4

(Release ID: 2286952)
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW  
The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare announced achievements in immunization coverage, pandemic preparedness, and public health infrastructure. Key highlights include sustained national immunization coverage above 90% for four consecutive years, the nationwide rollout of a free HPV vaccination campaign for 1.2 crore girls aged 14, and expanded surveillance infrastructure to address public health emergencies.  

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
- National Full Immunization Coverage (FIC) maintained at **94.7%** (2022-23), **93.7%** (2023-24), **98.3%** (2024-25), and **97.9%** (2025-26).  
- Nationwide HPV vaccination campaign launched on **28 February 2026** for **1.2 crore girls aged 14 years** across all 36 states and UTs.  
- **>160 Virus Research and Diagnostic Laboratories (VRDLs)** established by ICMR for pathogen diagnosis.

- **Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR)** expanded its network to **>160 VRDLs** for laboratory-based pathogen diagnosis.  
- **Oxygen infrastructure support**: Supply of oxygen cylinders, concentrators, and Liquid Medical Oxygen tanks; establishment of Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA) plants and medical gas pipeline systems.  
- **Integrated Health Information Platform (IHIP)**, **Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme (IDSP)**, **Health Management Information System (HMIS)**, and **Ayushman Arogya Mandir (AAM) Portal** operational for real-time data management and outbreak detection.

headline: Update on Availability of Cancer Care Centres
fullText:
Ministry of Health and Family Welfare

Update on Availability of Cancer Care Centres

Under NP-NCD, 770 District NCD Clinics, 524 Day Care Cancer Centres and 6,410 NCD clinics at Community Health Centres have been set up across the country

AB PM-JAY scheme covers cashless treatment for 1,961 procedures across 27 medical specialties, including Medical Oncology, Surgical Oncology and Radiation Oncology, through empanelled public and private hospitals

To reduce waiting time for cancer diagnosis and treatment, population-based screening for common NCDs, including oral, breast and cervical cancers, is being undertaken under the National Health Mission

Under the Strengthening of Tertiary Care Cancer Facilities Scheme, 19 State Cancer Institutes and 20 Tertiary Care Cancer Centres have been approved across the country

Tata Memorial Centre has established six hospitals at Varanasi, Visakhapatnam, New Chandigarh, Guwahati, Sangrur and Muzaffarpur to provide comprehensive cancer care

Cancer treatment services, including diagnostic, medical and surgical facilities have also been extended to all 22 new AIIMS

21 JUL 2026 1:54PM by PIB Delhi

Health being a State subject, the primary responsibility for providing healthcare services, including cancer care, rests with the respective State Governments. However, the Department of Health and Family Welfare provides technical and financial support to States and Union Territories across the country under the National Programme for Prevention and Control of Non-Communicable Diseases (NP-NCD) as part of the National Health Mission (NHM). The programme focuses on strengthening infrastructure, human resource development, screening, early diagnosis, referral, treatment, and health promotion for Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs). Under NP-NCD, 770 District NCD Clinics, 524 Day Care Cancer Centres (DCCCs) and 6,410 NCD clinics at Community Health Centres have been set up across the country.

Under Ayushman Bharat–Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY), eligible beneficiary families are provided health coverage of ₹5 lakh per family per year for secondary and tertiary care hospitalization. The scheme covers cashless treatment for 1,961 procedures across 27 medical specialties, including Medical Oncology, Surgical Oncology and Radiation Oncology, through empanelled public and private hospitals. States/UTs also have the flexibility to customize the Health Benefit Packages to address local healthcare needs.

To reduce waiting periods for cancer diagnosis and treatment, population-based screening for common non-communicable diseases, including oral, breast and cervical cancers, is being undertaken under the National Health Mission (NHM) for individuals aged 30 years and above through Ayushman Arogya Mandirs by trained frontline health workers (ASHAs and ANMs). This facilitates early detection and timely referral, thereby reducing delays in diagnosis and initiation of treatment. Further, DCCCs are being established in District Hospitals under a hub-and-spoke model to decentralize cancer care and facilitate timely access to chemotherapy and other day-care oncology services.

Under the Strengthening of Tertiary Care Cancer Facilities Scheme, 19 State Cancer Institutes (SCIs) and 20 Tertiary Care Cancer Centres (TCCCs) have been approved across the country to strengthen tertiary cancer care. Further, Tata Memorial Centre (TMC) has established six hospitals at Varanasi, Visakhapatnam, New Chandigarh, Guwahati, Sangrur and Muzaffarpur to provide comprehensive cancer care. Cancer treatment services, including diagnostic, medical and surgical facilities, have also been extended to all 22 new AIIMS. In addition, the National Cancer Institute (NCI), Jhajjar and the second campus of Chittaranjan National Cancer Institute (CNCI), Kolkata provide advanced diagnostic and treatment facilities.

The Union Minister of State for Health and Family Welfare, Shri Prataprao Jadhav stated this in a written reply in the Rajya Sabha today.

HFW/PQ-Update on Availability of Cancer Care Centres/21 July 2026/3

(Release ID: 2286948)
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW
The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare is strengthening cancer care infrastructure and accessibility through various national initiatives, including the NP-NCD, AB-PMJAY, and the Strengthening of Tertiary Care Cancer Facilities Scheme. These efforts focus on decentralizing care, improving early diagnosis through population-based screening, and expanding specialized medical services across the country.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS
* **NP-NCD Infrastructure:** 770 District NCD Clinics, 524 Day Care Cancer Centres, and 6,410 NCD clinics at Community Health Centres have been established nationwide.
* **AB-PMJAY Coverage:** Provides ₹5 lakh per family per year for secondary and tertiary care hospitalization, covering 1,961 procedures across 27 medical specialties, including Medical, Surgical, and Radiation Oncology.
* **Screening and Early Detection:** Population-based screening for oral, breast, and cervical cancers is being conducted for individuals aged 30 and above by trained frontline workers (ASHAs and ANMs) through Ayushman Arogya Mandirs.
* **Tertiary Care Expansion:** 19 State Cancer Institutes (SCIs) and 20 Tertiary Care Cancer Centres (TCCCs) have been approved under the Strengthening of Tertiary Care Cancer Facilities Scheme.
* **Tata Memorial Centre (TMC) Facilities:** Six hospitals have been established at Varanasi, Visakhapatnam, New Chandigarh, Guwahati, Sangrur, and Muzaffarpur to provide comprehensive cancer care.
* **AIIMS and Advanced Facilities:** Cancer treatment services have been extended to all 22 new AIIMS; additional advanced facilities are provided by the National Cancer Institute (NCI) in Jhajjar and the second campus of the Chittaranjan National Cancer Institute (CNCI) in Kolkata.

headline: Ministry I&B Launches Pilot Phase of 'Gems of India' Challenge to Spotlight Grassroots Digital Creators
fullText:
Ministry of Information & Broadcasting

Ministry I&B Launches Pilot Phase of 'Gems of India' Challenge to Spotlight Grassroots Digital Creators

Shoot, Upload, Shine: "Gems of India" Challenge Opens for Entries on MyWAVES from 1st August

States and District Administrations to Anchor the Challenge on Ground, Creator Mobilisation to Jury Evaluation

YouTube and Meta Partner the Challenge, Sharing their Creator Ecosystems

21 JUL 2026 1:52PM

As part of the Government's continued efforts to strengthen India's creative sector and promote the emerging Orange Economy, the Ministry of Information & Broadcasting today launched the Pilot Phase of the 'Gems of India' Challenge, a structured national initiative to identify, nurture and promote grassroots digital creators from across the country.

Under the visionary leadership of Hon’ble Prime Minister, India has embraced technology as a powerful instrument of empowerment, inclusion and nation-building. Initiatives such as Digital India have democratised access to technology and created opportunities for innovation at an unprecedented scale.

The initiative builds upon MyWAVES, the citizen creator platform under WAVES OTT, launched by Shri Ashwini Vaishnaw, Hon'ble Minister for Information & Broadcasting to enable citizens to create, upload and share original content while strengthening India's digital creator ecosystem. Envisioned as a platform to encourage user-generated content and showcase India's rich cultural diversity, MyWAVES now provides the foundation for the Gems of India Challenge, which invites creators to tell the unique stories of their districts through authentic digital storytelling.

Secretary, Ministry of Information & Broadcasting, Shri Chanchal Kumar acknowledged that “Every district of India has a story waiting to be told, and Gems of India gives our creators the platform to tell it. This is as much about celebrating our youth's creativity as it is about celebrating the extraordinary diversity of our districts.”

The Challenge seeks to create a vibrant nationwide network of creators by encouraging them to showcase the culture, heritage, tourism, traditions, history and distinctive achievements of their districts through authentic digital storytelling, while celebrating the unique identity of every district in India.

How to Participate: To join the challenge, creators can submit videos of 1 to 3 minutes in duration via the MyWAVES section on the WAVES OTT platform. The submission window for the Pilot Phase will open on August 1, 2026, and will remain open until August 31, 2026.

Pilot Phase Coverage and Partnerships: The Pilot Phase is being launched across Andaman & Nicobar Islands, Arunachal Pradesh, Chandigarh, Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu, Goa, and Ladakh. This phase is a collaborative effort involving Prasar Bharati, My Bharat, and the respective State and District Administrations. Local cultural and educational institutions will also be actively involved to facilitate creator mobilization and content development.

To further amplify the initiative, the Ministry is collaborating with industry giants YouTube and Meta. Both platforms will help mobilize their creator communities and extend the outreach of the challenge across their networks.

Awards and Recognition: To honour outstanding talent, the program will confer prestigious awards at the District, State, and National levels:

District Level: Up to 20 awards of ₹50,000 each per district.

State Level: One exceptional creator from each district will be selected for a State-level Award of ₹2 lakh.

National Level: During the subsequent national rollout, selected State entries for National Awards of ₹5 lakh each.

To ensure fair evaluation, the District and State Jury Committees will be constituted by the respective administration. These panels will comprise eminent personalities from the fields of culture, heritage, tourism, media, and academia.

Future Nationwide Rollout: Based on the insights and experience gained during this pilot phase, the Gems of India Challenge will eventually expand to cover all States and Union Territories. The ultimate goal is to establish a vibrant, nationwide ecosystem of digital creators who celebrate the unique identity and heritage of every district in India.

Mahesh Kumar/Vivek Vishwash/Aparajita Priyadarshini

| Visitor Counter:
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW  
The Ministry of Information & Broadcasting has launched the pilot phase of the “Gems of India” Challenge, a national program that invites grassroots digital creators to showcase their district’s culture and heritage on the MyWAVES platform. The pilot runs from 1 August to 31 August 2026 in selected districts and partners with YouTube, Meta, Prasar Bharati, and other state bodies to support content creation and award selection.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS  

- **Pilot Phase launch date:** 1 August 2026  
- **Submission window:** 1 August 2026 – 31 August 2026  
- **Video entry requirements:** 1–3 minute original videos uploaded via MyWAVES on WAVES OTT.  
- **Pilot coverage:** Andaman & Nicobar Islands, Arunachal Pradesh, Chandigarh, Dadra & Nagar Haveli & Daman & Diu, Goa, and Ladakh.  
- **Partnerships:** Collaboration with Prasar Bharati, My Bharat, YouTube, Meta, and local cultural and educational institutions.  
- **Award structure:**  
  - *District Level:* Up to 20 awards of ₹50,000 each per district.  
  - *State Level:* One creator per district selected for a State Award of ₹2 lakh.  
  - *National Level:* Subsequent national rollout will award ₹5 lakh each to selected State entries.  
- **Evaluation mechanism:** District and State jury committees, comprising experts in culture, heritage, tourism, media, and academia, will judge entries.  
- **Long‑term goal:** Expansion of the challenge to all States tristique Union Territories to build a nationwide ecosystem of digital creators celebrating district identities.

headline: Update on HPV Vaccination Programme and Cervical Cancer Prevention
fullText:
Ministry of Health and Family Welfare

Update on HPV Vaccination Programme and Cervical Cancer Prevention

As of 15 July 2026, a total of 52,10,302 girls have received the HPV vaccine across the country

Extensive training on HPV vaccination has been completed for medical and paramedical staff across all 36 States/UTs in the country

A multi-faceted approach that includes HPV vaccination, screening, early diagnosis at Ayushman Arogya Mandirs and treatment through Day Care Cancer Centres is implemented by the Union Government against Cervical Cancer

21 JUL 2026 1:52PM by PIB Delhi

The Nationwide HPV vaccination campaign was launched on 28th February 2026 for approximately 1.2 crore eligible beneficiary girls aged 14 years across all 36 States and Union Territories. As of 15 July 2026, a total of 52,10,302 girls have received the HPV vaccine across the country. The HPV vaccine is delivered free of cost at designated government health facilities, including Ayushman Arogya Mandirs- Primary Health Centres (PHCs), Community Health Centers (CHCs), Sub-District Hospitals (SDH)/District Hospitals (DHs) & Government Medical Colleges (GMCs).

The extensive training on HPV vaccination has been completed for medical and paramedical staff across all 36 States/UTs in the country. The coverage data on the HPV vaccination campaign is captured through U-WIN portal, the digital vaccination platform of the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare. Awareness generation is promoted through IEC materials such as radio jingles, posters, banners, myth-busting digital content and social media platforms to address vaccine hesitancy and ensure high vaccine uptake among girls particularly in rural, tribal, and urban slum areas.

The Government of India has implemented comprehensive prevention strategies against cervical cancer, encompassing a multi-faceted approach that includes HPV vaccination, screening, early diagnosis at Ayushman Arogya Mandirs (AAMs), and treatment through Day Care Cancer Centres (DCCCs).

The Union Minister of State for Health and Family Welfare, Smt. Anupriya Patel stated this in a written reply in the Rajya Sabha today.

HFW/PQ-Update on Cervical Cancer Care and Prevention/21 July 2026/1

(Release ID: 2286947)

As of 15 July 2026, 5,210,302 girls have received the HPV vaccine under a nationwide campaign launched on 28 February 2026 targeting approximately 12 million 14‑year‑old girls. The government also outlined its comprehensive cervical cancer prevention strategy that combines vaccination, screening, early diagnosis, and treatment.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS

- Vaccination coverage data captured via the U‑WIN portal (digital vaccination platform)  
- Awareness generation through IEC materials: radio jingles, posters, banners, myth‑busting digital content, social media to address vaccine hesitancy, with focus on rural, tribal, and urban slum areas  
- Cervical cancer prevention approach: HPV vaccination + screening + early diagnosis at Ayushman Arogya Mandirs + treatment through Day Care Cancer Centres

headline: Ministry of Coal to Release AAROH, Sign India-Germany Implementation Agreement in New Delhi on 22nd July, 2026
fullText:
Ministry of Coal

Ministry of Coal to Release AAROH, Sign India-Germany Implementation Agreement in New Delhi on 22nd July, 2026

The Event to also Witness Inauguration of Coal NEER Plants and Signing of MoU for Skill Development under Revised Jharia Master Plan

21 JUL 2026 1:00PM by PIB Delhi

Building on its sustained efforts to promote responsible mine closure practices, the Ministry of Coal is set to release 'AAROH – Annual Report on Mine Closure' in New Delhi on 22nd July 2026. For the first time since independence, 42 coal mines have been scientifically closed, in accordance with approved mine closure plans. The report presents the transformative journey of these closed mines, showcasing how scientific reclamation, ecological restoration, sustainable post-mining land utilisation, and community-centric interventions have enabled the productive reuse of former mining areas.

The event will be graced by Shri G. Kishan Reddy, Union Minister of Coal and Mines, as the Chief Guest. Shri Bhupender Yadav, Union Minister for Environment, Forest and Climate Change and Dr. Jitendra Singh, Union Minister of State (Independent Charge) for Science & Technology, Earth Sciences, and Minister of State in the Prime Minister's Office, Personnel, Public Grievances & Pensions, Department of Atomic Energy and Department of Space, will attend as the Special Guest while Shri Satish Chandra Dubey, Union Minister of State for Coal and Mines, will be the Guest of Honour. Shri Vikram Dev Dutt, Secretary, Ministry of Coal, Shri Sanoj Kumar Jha, Additional Secretary, Ministry of Coal, Shri Sajeesh Kumar N., Coal Controller and other senior officials of the Ministry will also be present on the occasion.

Mine closure is a critical stage in the mining lifecycle, encompassing ecological restoration, land reclamation, and the productive repurposing of post-mining sites for community and economic benefit. Recognizing its importance, the Ministry of Coal has, over recent years, developed a series of frameworks, including the RECLAIM Framework for community engagement, L.I.V.E.S. Framework for sustainable mine closure & repurposing, and SUVIKALP (an interactive decision support tool) for assessing mine repurposing options, to guide scientific closure, stakeholder participation, and sustainable land-use planning across the coalfields.

Reflecting this evolving approach, the programme will showcase on-ground innovations in mine reclamation, ecological restoration, and community-oriented rehabilitation, with an accompanying exhibition featuring initiatives undertaken by Coal India Limited and its subsidiaries, NLC India Limited, Singareni Collieries Company Limited (SCCL), NTPC, and private mining organisations. The inauguration of Coal NEER Plants set up by Coal India Limited through its subsidiaries, aimed at strengthening access to safe and sustainable drinking water in coal-bearing regions, will further reinforce this commitment toward community-focused development.

The occasion will also mark significant steps towards strengthening institutional partnerships and international cooperation to advance scientific mine closure and sustainable post-mining land use practices.

The Ministry of Coal will sign an Implementation Agreement with Deutsche Gesellschaft für Internationale Zusammenarbeit (GIZ) to foster technical cooperation on ‘to-be closed coal mines’. This collaboration aims to strengthen institutional capacity, facilitate knowledge sharing, and support the adoption of international best practices in scientific mine closure and post-mining development.

The event will also witness the signing of Tripartite Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs) among Bharat Coking Coal Limited (BCCL), the Jharia Rehabilitation and Development Authority (JRDA), and Hindalco Industries Limited and Rajdhani Universal Fabrics Private Limited for establishing vocational training facilities under the Revised Jharia Master Plan.

Through these efforts, the Ministry of Coal reaffirms its continued commitment to integrating sustainability, innovation and community-centric development into mine closure planning. Through structured frameworks, digital tools and collaborative stakeholder platforms, the Ministry is paving the way for transforming mine closure into an opportunity for ecological restoration, economic diversification and long-term regional development.

(Release ID: 2286931)

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS

headline: Tourism Ministry Sanctions 117 Projects Worth ₹5,756.62 Crore to Develop Tourism Infrastructure
fullText:
Ministry of Tourism

Tourism Ministry Sanctions 117 Projects Worth ₹5,756.62 Crore to Develop Tourism Infrastructure

21 JUL 2026 12:13PM by PIB Delhi

The Ministry of Tourism has undertaken several initiatives during the last two years to promote tourism through infrastructure development, digital promotion and expansion of basic amenities across the country.

The Ministry provides Central Financial Assistance to State Governments and Union Territory Administrations under the schemes of Swadesh Darshan 2.0, Challenge Based Destination Development, Pilgrimage Rejuvenation and Spiritual Heritage Augmentation Drive (PRASHAD), Assistance to Central Agencies for Tourism Infrastructure Development and Dharti Aaba Janjatiya Gram Utkarsh Abhiyan (DA JUGA) for development of tourism infrastructure and augmentation of basic amenities, subject to the scheme guidelines and availability of funds. Further, under the Government of India initiative Special Assistance to States for Capital Investment – Development of Iconic Tourist Centres to Global Scale, financial assistance was provided to States during 2024-25 for tourism projects.

In the last two years, the Ministry has sanctioned 117 projects worth Rs. 5756.62 crores, under the afore-mentioned Schemes. The State/UT-wise details of Central Financial Assistance sanctioned under these schemes during the last two financial years are given at Annexure.

The Ministry promotes tourism through the Incredible India Digital Platform, comprising the website and mobile application, which serves as a one-stop digital interface for domestic and international travellers. The revamped portal, launched in September 2024, is available in multiple languages and offers curated travel content, essential information, and seamless booking of flights, hotels, cabs, buses, and ASI monument tickets through partnerships with Online Travel Agents (OTAs) and other stakeholders. Promotion is further supported through official social media platforms, digital campaigns, and collaborations with social media influencers.

The Ek Bharat Shreshtha Bharat (EBSB) initiative is implemented by the Ministry of Education, Government of India, the nodal Ministry for the EBSB. However, under the EBSB initiative, the Ministry of Tourism is also implementing several programmes through tourism events and institutional activities. The Ministry organises its flagship event Bharat Parv every year as part of the Republic Day celebrations with participation from all States and Union Territories to showcase India's cultural heritage, tourism potential and diverse traditions. Under this initiative, the Ministry also participated in Rashtriya Ekta Diwas 2025 at Ekta Nagar, Gujarat.

Further, the Ministry's Central Autonomous Bodies, including 21 Institutes of Hotel Management (IHMs), the Indian Institute of Tourism and Travel Management (IITTM), the Indian Culinary Institute (ICI) and India Tourism Offices regularly organise student exchange programmes, familiarisation visits, tour operator networking sessions, cultural programmes, regional cuisine festivals, language-learning activities and other activities highlighting the culture, traditions, cuisine and heritage of paired States and Union Territories under the EBSB initiative. These initiatives promote cultural tourism, foster people-to-people connect, strengthen national integration and reinforce the spirit of national pride.

The Ministry has taken several measures to enhance international tourist arrivals, improve ease of travel and promote niche tourism products. India is promoted in key international source markets through tourism roadshows, trade meetings, familiarisation visits, media and social media campaigns, food festivals and participation in major international tourism fairs and exhibitions in collaboration with Indian Missions, States/UTs and tourism stakeholders.

To improve ease of travel, the Government has liberalised and simplified the visa regime through the e-Visa facility, which is currently available under nine sub-categories for nationals of 174 countries. The Ministry also provides a 24x7 Multi-lingual Tourist Helpline and has also introduced the Incredible India Tourist Facilitator Certification (IITFC) Programme to create a pool of professionally trained tourist facilitators and guides, while existing Regional Level Guides have been integrated into the Incredible India Tourist Guide (IITG) system with pan-India recognition. Further, the Ministry is working with the Ministry of Civil Aviation to improve connectivity to tourist destinations under the Regional Connectivity Scheme (RCS-UDAN), under which 53 tourism routes have been identified, and regularly coordinates with the Ministry of Home Affairs on measures relating to travel in Restricted and Protected Areas to facilitate seamless travel for tourists.

The Ministry promotes niche tourism products such as spiritual, eco, adventure, rural, tribal, wellness, medical, MICE, wedding and heritage tourism through policy initiatives, infrastructure development and promotional activities. It has formulated National Strategies for Sustainable Tourism, Rural Tourism, Rural Homestays, MICE, Adventure Tourism, Eco-tourism and Medical & Wellness Tourism for implementation by States/UTs. The Ministry has also launched the "Meet in India" sub-brand for MICE tourism, the "India Says I Do" campaign for destination weddings and issued the Model Adventure Tourism Safety Framework for safe and sustainable adventure tourism.

The written reply was given by the Minister of Tourism Shri Gajendra Singh Shekhawat in the Lok Sabha on 20.07.2026.

headline: Journeys of Grit, Pathways of Growth
fullText:
PIB Backgrounder

Journeys of Grit, Pathways of Growth

RSETIs Empowering Rural Aspirations through Enterprise

21 JUL 2026 11:51AM by PIB Delhi

Vishnu Bai reflects on a belief that has shaped her journey: that those who rise find the world lifting them further. She was born into a modest farming family, where dreams rarely stretched beyond daily survival. Her schooling stopped after eighth standard, cut short by an early marriage and new responsibilities. Within eighteen months, she returned home, as her marital household sank under financial strain. When her brothers refused to provide financial support, she chose self-reliance and began stitching clothes at home. Each small earning carried not just income, but a fragile sense of independence.

Her turning point came through the Shri Ram Self-Help Group, which introduced her to training opportunities at the Rural Self Employment Training Institute (RSETI) in Bhopal. She wanted to learn tailoring alongside other women, but social barriers stood firm. Facing mobility restrictions, she requested training in her village. The institute responded, and the Panchayat Bhawan became a classroom of hope. Women gathered there, learning stitching alongside the basics of running a small business. For many, it was their first step into a world beyond household boundaries.

After completing training, Vishnu Bai opened a modest tailoring shop in her village.

Her work slowly expanded to include men’s garments. A government order for school uniforms brought steady income and renewed confidence. Soon after, she owned industrial sewing machines and built a stable livelihood through her growing enterprise. Her journey reflects quiet courage, shaped not by sudden change but persistent determination. Stories like hers echo across rural India, where transformation often unfolds without noise or spectacle.

RSETIs: The Bridge from Aspirations to Enterprise

At the centre of this transformation are Rural Self Employment Training Institutes (RSETIs) under the Deendayal Antyodaya Yojana-National Rural Livelihoods Mission (DAY-NRLM), working steadily in the background to promote self-employment and nurture micro-enterprises. These district-level institutes offer free, short-term, practical, residential training to unemployed rural people between eighteen and fifty years of age. Through three-way partnerships linking the Ministry of Rural Development, State/UT governments, and sponsor banks, their role extends beyond classrooms, reaching into livelihoods through credit support and continued guidance.

Seventy-three courses span agriculture, manufacturing, services, and entrepreneurship and align with national skill standards. Sponsor banks play a crucial role, ensuring trainees have access to credit to build viable enterprises. This integrated approach ensures that skills do not remain theoretical but translate into real economic activity. For many, this support system becomes the turning point where possibility begins to take shape.

Another such journey of Umesh Shivaji Ingle from daily wager to employment generator reflects how RSETIs reshape lives with strength. Coming from a financially constrained family, he could study only up to the tenth standard. For nearly five years, he worked as a daily wager in a noodle factory. Despite long hours, his earnings barely sustained his household’s needs. A friend’s suggestion introduced him to the RSETI training programme, opening an unexpected door. He enrolled in an Entrepreneurship Development Programme, drawing on his factory experience to deepen his learning. After training, he started a small noodle unit with a second-hand machine and basic tools. At first, he produced around 1,000 to 1,200 kilograms of noodles daily. Gradually, demand grew, and his enterprise began earning a monthly profit of about thirty thousand rupees. He employed four women from vulnerable backgrounds, extending opportunity beyond his own family. A loan of eight lakh rupees from the bank helped him scale his operations. He shifted to a larger unit in MIDC Akola, expanding production to meet rising demand. From a daily wager to an employer, his journey had redefined purpose by creating livelihoods for others.

While some journeys begin with economic needs, others emerge from creative aspirations. In Dharwad, Sanjay Malagi’s story reflects how RSETIs help diverse dreams grow into enterprises. After completing a diploma in electrical engineering, he worked in a multinational company in Bengaluru. Over time, dissatisfaction grew, urging him to leave and return home in search of purpose. During this uncertain phase, he discovered a photography course offered by RSETI Dharwad. He enrolled, and what began as curiosity soon turned into a clear entrepreneurial vision. In 2019, he founded his company, Cinewinks, stepping into a world of storytelling. His work spans videography, editing, and direction, weaving narratives across multiple Indian languages. His films explore migration, politics, and technology misuse with striking emotional depth.

His film ‘Tamasoma Jyotirgamaya’ earned two international awards for portraying the struggles of weavers. Another work, ‘Hinnota’, brought him the Best Director award at Cochin International Short Film Festival in the year 2020-21. Over time, he won nearly ten awards, gaining recognition for his creative voice. His company began operating across multiple cities and undertaking diverse professional projects, while also generating local employment.

Together, these journeys reveal a larger story unfolding across the country.

They reflect not isolated success, but a growing ecosystem of resilience and opportunity. As of March 2026, 632 RSETIs operate across 33 States/UTs. They cover 619 districts and are supported by 25 sponsor banks nationwide. Since inception in 2009 until February 2026, RSETIs have demonstrated sustained and impactful progress nationwide. Against a training target of 60.81 lakh, about 99.7% has been achieved, with 60.63 lakh candidates trained. Of these, 43.89 lakh candidates have secured sustainable livelihoods. Institutional support has further enabled 22.52 lakh beneficiaries to access bank finance. Recent years have witnessed considerable growth, indicating expanding outreach and improved institutional effectiveness. Notably, 2024-25 recorded the highest annual training achievement, with 6.21 lakh candidates trained.

These numbers tell a story, but the deeper narrative lies in human transformation.

RSETIs are not merely training centres, but spaces where aspiration takes root. Across villages, small enterprises are reshaping local economies and restoring dignity. With every success story, confidence replaces hesitation, and hope becomes tangible. In these quiet shifts, rural India moves steadily towards a future shaped by self-reliance and belief.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS

headline: Digital India BHASHINI Division Brings ‘BHASHINI Rajyam’ to Goa to Advance AI-Powered Governance in Konkani and Indian Languages
fullText:
Ministry of Electronics & IT

Digital India BHASHINI Division Brings ‘BHASHINI Rajyam’ to Goa to Advance AI-Powered Governance in Konkani and Indian Languages

Chief Minister Dr. Pramod Sawant, IT Minister Shri Rohan A. Khaunte and Senior Leaders Join State Engagement Workshop on Multilingual AI

21 JUL 2026 11:28AM by PIB Delhi

The Digital India BHASHINI Division (DIBD), under Digital India Corporation (DIC), Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY), Government of India, in collaboration with the Department of Information Technology, Electronics & Communications, Government of Goa, organised the 'BHASHINI Rajyam: State Engagement Workshop' in Panaji to advance multilingual AI and strengthen language-first Digital Public Infrastructure. The event brought together policymakers, senior government officials, industry leaders, academia, startups and language experts, and was attended by Chief Minister Dr. Pramod Sawant and Minister for Information Technology, Electronics & Communications, Tourism, Printing & Stationery Shri Rohan A. Khaunte, reflecting a shared commitment to making digital governance more inclusive, accessible and multilingual.

With a special focus on Konkani, the workshop highlighted how AI-powered language technologies can make digital governance more inclusive by enabling citizens to access government services, information and digital platforms in their preferred language. The discussions underscored the role of multilingual AI in building a voice-first ecosystem where language is no longer a barrier to public service delivery.

The inaugural session featured addresses by Shri Santosh Sukhdve, IAS, Secretary, Department of Information Technology, Electronics & Communications, Government of Goa; Shri Prasad Lolayekar, IAS, Secretary, Higher Education, Government of Goa; Dr. Chandrakant Shetye, MLA, Bicholim and Chairman, InfoTech Corporation of Goa Ltd.; Shri Rohan A. Khaunte, Minister for Information Technology, Electronics & Communications, Tourism, Printing & Stationery, Government of Goa; and Dr. Pramod Sawant, Chief Minister of Goa.

Delivering the keynote address, Shri Amitabh Nag, CEO, Digital India BHASHINI Division, outlined BHASHINI's vision for a language-first Digital Public Infrastructure powered by multilingual AI. He highlighted its growing impact across governance, tourism, healthcare, education, banking and citizen services, and called for stronger collaboration among governments, academia, startups and language experts to strengthen regional language ecosystems, including Konkani, and drive inclusive digital transformation.

The workshop featured technical sessions on the National Hub for Language Technology (NHLT) and showcased BHASHINI's growing ecosystem of multilingual AI solutions. Participants were introduced to BHASHINI App Mitra, BHASHINI Mitra and BHASHINI Pravakta, demonstrating how AI-powered translation, speech recognition, text-to-speech, transliteration and multilingual APIs can be seamlessly integrated into government applications and citizen-facing digital services.

The BHASHINI team showcased live demonstrations of its AI-powered language solutions, offering participants a first-hand experience of multilingual speech, translation, transcription and document-based technologies. The demonstrations highlighted how BHASHINI can strengthen citizen-centric governance while enabling seamless multilingual access to tourism services, public information and digital platforms, enhancing digital experiences for both citizens and visitors.

A dedicated session focused on strengthening the Konkani language ecosystem through collaboration between government, academia, language experts and technology partners. Discussions explored opportunities to expand language datasets, enhance AI models and identify state-specific use cases to accelerate the adoption of multilingual AI across Goa.

The Open Mic on Use Cases fostered discussions on state-specific applications of multilingual AI across governance, tourism, education, healthcare and citizen services, while exploring opportunities to integrate BHASHINI across Goa's digital platforms for more inclusive, language-first public services.

The workshop reaffirmed BHASHINI's commitment to working closely with State Governments to build language-first Digital Public Infrastructure. It marked the beginning of deeper collaboration with the Government of Goa to strengthen the Konkani language ecosystem, identify priority use cases and expand the adoption of multilingual AI across government services, enabling more accessible, inclusive and citizen-centric governance.

Shri Rohan A. Khaunte, Minister for Information Technology, Electronics & Communications, Tourism, Printing & Stationery, Government of Goa, said:“The BHASHINI Rajyam Workshop is a significant step towards building an inclusive, AI-powered digital Goa. Through our collaboration with the Digital India BHASHINI Division, we aim to remove language barriers and ensure that every citizen—including those in the remotest parts of Goa—can access digital services in Konkani. As AI becomes more accessible, preserving and empowering our language is central to Goa's digital future and innovation journey."

Shri Amitabh Nag, CEO, Digital India BHASHINI Division, said: "A comprehensive demonstration showcased a simulated voice journey of a Goan youth, highlighting how BHASHINI's multilingual AI ecosystem can enable seamless interactions with governance and public services through voice. The persona-based journey illustrated key touchpoints—from accessing government schemes and digital services to education and citizen support—demonstrating how AI-powered speech technologies can make service delivery more inclusive, accessible, and language-first. The session also highlighted the importance of developing high-quality Konkani language datasets to strengthen AI capabilities and accelerate the creation of voice-enabled digital public services in the language."

About Digital India BHASHINI Division (DIBD)

The Digital India BHASHINI Division (DIBD), under Digital India Corporation (DIC), MeitY, is India's national platform for AI-powered multilingual digital inclusion. BHASHINI powers 800+ government websites, has enabled 8+ billion AI inferences, processes 20+ million AI inferences daily, and supports 36 Indian text languages, 23 Indian voice languages and 35 international languages, making Digital Public Infrastructure more accessible through language AI.

Dharmendra Tewari / Dr. Nayan Solanki / Kanishk Sharma / Vanshita Jaiswal

headline: ICMR transfers three indigenous biomedical technologies to advance cancer and infectious disease solutions
fullText:
Ministry of Health and Family Welfare

ICMR transfers three indigenous biomedical technologies to advance cancer and infectious disease solutions

SHetA2 Anti-HPV candidate, two enteric vaccines among technologies transferred for further development

Technology transfer brings government-funded research closer to affordable healthcare

Medical Innovations Patent Mitra driving India's path to self-reliance in healthcare innovation: Secretary DHR and DG ICMR, Dr. Rajiv Bahl

21 JUL 2026 11:26AM by PIB Delhi

The Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), under its Medical Innovations Patent Mitra initiative, facilitated the licensing of three indigenous biomedical technologies to leading Indian pharmaceutical and vaccine manufacturers for further development, manufacturing and commercialization. The technologies include SHetA2, a novel anti-HPV therapeutic candidate for the treatment of Cervical Intraepithelial Neoplasia (CIN), licensed to Emcure Pharmaceuticals Ltd., and two next-generation vaccine technologies against enteric bacterial infections, developed at ICMR–National Institute of Research in Bacterial Infections (ICMR-NIRBI), Kolkata, licensed to Biological E Ltd. These technology transfers mark another significant step towards accelerating the translation of publicly funded research into affordable healthcare solutions and strengthening India's biomedical innovation ecosystem.

SHetA2, developed through a collaboration between Dr. Showket Hussain, ICMR National Institute of Cancer Prevention and Research (ICMR-NICPR) and Dr. Doris M. Benbrook, Stephenson Cancer Center, University of Oklahoma, USA, is a first-in-class targeted therapeutic candidate that selectively eliminates HPV-induced precancerous and cervical cancer cells while sparing healthy cells. The technology has the potential to provide a safe, affordable and minimally invasive treatment option for cervical cancer.

The two vaccine technologies developed by Dr. Santasabuj Das and his team at ICMR National Institute for Research in Bacterial Infections (ICMR-NIRBI) include:

A fusion construct containing Salmonella typhi outer membrane protein as a promising next-generation typhoid vaccine candidate.

A recombinant vaccine composition against Salmonella typhi/paratyphi and Shigella infections, designed to provide broader protection against major enteric bacterial pathogens.

Together, these technologies have the potential to strengthen India's efforts in cancer prevention and infectious disease control while advancing indigenous development of next-generation therapeutics and vaccines.

Dr. Rajiv Bahl, Secretary, Department of Health Research (DHR) and Director General, ICMR, said, "ICMR is committed to ensuring that publicly funded research translates into products that improve health outcomes. Through initiatives such as Medical Innovations Patent Mitra, we are strengthening the pathway from scientific discovery to commercialization by fostering partnerships between researchers and industry. These technology transfers demonstrate the growing maturity of India's biomedical innovation ecosystem and represent another step towards achieving self-reliance in healthcare innovation."

As India's apex biomedical research organization, ICMR has established an integrated innovation ecosystem that supports healthcare technologies across the innovation continuum—from discovery and intellectual property protection to clinical validation, regulatory facilitation and commercialization. Through Medical Innovations Patent Mitra, innovators receive structured support for patent filing, technology transfer and licensing, while MedTech Mitra facilitates regulatory guidance, clinical evaluation and market access for indigenous medical technologies.

The licensing of these technologies reflects the growing strength of India's biomedical innovation ecosystem, where scientific excellence, strategic collaborations and industry partnerships are enabling affordable, high-quality healthcare solutions for India and the world.

It is for information of all stakeholders/public concerned that the Supreme Court of India, vide its Order dated 25.05.2026 in Suo Motu Writ Petition (Civil) No. 10 of 2025, constituted a High-Powered Committee (HPC) to examine matters related to the Aravalli Hills and Ranges, besides Ancillary issues, and submit its comprehensive report to the apex Court before 31.08.2026.

Submissions from all stakeholders, to the extent possible supported by documentary evidence or other verifiable material, should be communicated through email at mshpcaravalli[at]gmail[dot]com or through google forms specifically designed for the purpose (link: https://forms.gle/FpWwXBES5Gfr9bn16), within 21 days from the date of publication of this notice.

fullText:

CDSCO Clears Recombinant Live Attenuated Dengue Vaccine for Prevention of Dengue in Individuals Aged 4–60 Years

Dengue Vaccine Approved in 42 Countries with WHO Prequalification and Strong Global Safety Record

21 JUL 2026 8:35AM by PIB Delhi

In a Landmark step towards strengthening India's efforts for the prevention and control of dengue, the Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO), under the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, has granted marketing authorisation for the Dengue Tetravalent Vaccine (Live, Attenuated) – Qdenga®, manufactured by Takeda GmbH, Germany and to be imported by M/s Takeda Biopharmaceuticals India Pvt. Ltd. This is the first dengue vaccine approved in the country for the prevention of dengue disease.

The approval has been granted after a comprehensive scientific evaluation of the vaccine's quality, safety and efficacy in accordance with the provisions of the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940 and the Drugs Rules, 1945.

Qdenga® is a live, attenuated tetravalent dengue vaccine developed using recombinant DNA technology. The vaccine is produced in Vero cells, with genes encoding serotype-specific surface proteins engineered into a dengue virus type-2 backbone. The product contains genetically modified organisms (GMOs). It is supplied as a freeze-dried powder for reconstitution and is administered as a subcutaneous injection.

The vaccine is indicated for the prevention of dengue disease in individuals 4 to 60 years of age. The recommended immunisation schedule consists of two doses of 0.5 ml each, administered at an interval of three months (0 and 3 months).

The approval is supported by evidence generated through an extensive global clinical development programme evaluating the vaccine's safety, immunogenicity and efficacy across both dengue-endemic and non-endemic regions. Clinical studies were conducted in participants from more than eight countries, including Brazil, Thailand and Sri Lanka. The evaluation also included a pivotal Phase III safety and immunogenicity study conducted in the Indian population covering individuals aged 4 to 60 years.

The vaccine has already received regulatory approval in 42 countries, including the European Union, the United Kingdom, Switzerland, Indonesia, Malaysia and Thailand, and has also received World Health Organization (WHO) prequalification. Globally, more than 24 million doses of the vaccine have been distributed. Post-marketing surveillance data from countries where the vaccine is in use have demonstrated a favourable safety profile, with no significant safety concerns identified.

The approval of the country's first dengue vaccine marks an important milestone in India's public health response to dengue and is expected to complement the ongoing vector control, surveillance, early diagnosis and case management measures being implemented under the National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme.

The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare remains committed to ensuring timely access to safe, effective and quality-assured vaccines and medicines through a robust and science-based regulatory framework.

headline: India to Host the Sixteenth BRICS Health Ministers' Meeting in Chandigarh from 22–24 July
fullText:
Ministry of Health and Family Welfare

India to Host the Sixteenth BRICS Health Ministers' Meeting in Chandigarh from 22–24 July

Three-Day BRICS Health Programme to Feature Senior Officials' Meeting, Health Ministers' Meeting and Dialogue on Digital Health & AI

Union Health Secretary and Secretary, Ministry of Ayush Brief Media on Preparations for the Sixteenth BRICS Health Ministers' Meeting

India's BRICS Chairship Guided by Resilience, Innovation, Cooperation and Sustainability

Nine Priority Areas to Guide BRICS Health Cooperation under India's Chairship

Health Ministerial Declaration to Reinforce BRICS Commitment to Global Health

21 JUL 2026 7:18PM by PIB Delhi

As part of its BRICS Chairship 2026, India is set to convene the Sixteenth BRICS Health Ministers' Meeting in Chandigarh from 22–24 July 2026, bringing together Health Ministers and senior health officials from BRICS member countries to deepen cooperation on health security, digital health, pandemic preparedness and equitable access to quality healthcare.

Ahead of the Ministerial Meeting, a press conference was held in Chandigarh today, where Smt. Punya Salila Srivastava, Union Health Secretary, Union Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, and Shri Vaidya Rajesh Kotecha, Secretary, Ministry of Ayush, briefed the media on the preparations, agenda and expected outcomes of the Sixteenth BRICS Health Ministers' Meeting.

Addressing the media, the Union Health Secretary highlighted that the meeting reflects India's commitment, under its BRICS Chairship 2026, to advancing collective action on key global health priorities through the guiding theme of "Building for Resilience, Innovation, Cooperation and Sustainability." She emphasized that discussions during the Ministerial Meeting will focus on strengthening cooperation in areas such as health security, pandemic preparedness, digital health, innovation, access to affordable medicines, traditional, complementary and integrative medicine, tuberculosis, healthy lifestyles, mental wellness and resilient health systems, while furthering the shared objective of achieving Universal Health Coverage and improving health outcomes across BRICS countries. She also noted that the meeting would provide an important platform for advancing the health priorities of the Global South through enhanced collaboration and knowledge sharing among BRICS nations.

This marks India's fourth BRICS Chairship, following its earlier tenures in 2012, 2016 and 2021. Guided by the Chairship theme, "Building for Resilience, Innovation, Cooperation and Sustainability," India has placed health cooperation among the key pillars of its BRICS agenda, reflecting its commitment to advancing a people-centric and inclusive global health architecture.

The meeting will bring together Health Ministers, senior government officials and technical experts from the eleven BRICS member countries—Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa, Egypt, Ethiopia, Iran, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates and Indonesia. Together, these countries represent nearly half of the world's population and around 40 per cent of the global GDP, underscoring BRICS' growing role in shaping global health cooperation.

The official programme will commence with the Senior Officials' Meeting on 22 July 2026, where senior health officials will deliberate on the priority areas under the BRICS Health Track and finalise the draft Ministerial Declaration.

This will be followed by the Sixteenth BRICS Health Ministers' Meeting on 23 July 2026, chaired by Shri Jagat Prakash Nadda, Union Minister of Health and Family Welfare, during which Health Ministers will deliberate on key global and regional public health priorities and consider the adoption of the Sixteenth BRICS Health Ministers' Declaration.

On 24 July 2026, India will host a high-level side event titled "BRICS Dialogue: From Digital Health Records to AI-Enabled Healthcare – Strengthening the Continuum of Care through Interoperable Data Infrastructure and Responsible Artificial Intelligence," focusing on leveraging interoperable digital health infrastructure and responsible artificial intelligence to strengthen continuity of care and build future-ready health systems across BRICS countries.

Building upon the work undertaken during previous BRICS Chairships, India has retained the existing priorities while introducing two new priority areas—BRICS Mission for Healthy Lifestyle and Promotion of Mental Wellness, reaffirming its commitment to preventive and promotive healthcare.

Under its BRICS Chairship 2026, India has structured the BRICS Health Track around the following nine priority areas:

BRICS TB Research Network

Collaboration among BRICS Medical Products Regulatory Authorities

BRICS Integrated Early Warning System for Prevention and Response to Mass Infectious Diseases

Digital Health Architecture for Continuum of Care, including Access to Healthcare in Remote Areas

BRICS Mission for Healthy Lifestyle

Promotion of Mental Wellness

Traditional, Complementary and Integrative Medicine (TCIM)

Fight against Diseases Driven by Social Determinants of Health (DD-SDH)

BRICS Network of National Public Health Institutes

These priority areas reflect the collective commitment of BRICS countries to strengthening resilient health systems, promoting innovation, enhancing pandemic preparedness, addressing communicable and non-communicable diseases, expanding equitable access to quality healthcare, and accelerating progress towards Universal Health Coverage.

Beginning on 15th April 2026, India convened 26 technical meetings and expert consultations across these priority areas, bringing together experts from BRICS member countries to build consensus on key health issues.

The Sixteenth BRICS Health Ministers' Meeting is expected to strengthen collaboration among BRICS countries in the areas of health security, pandemic preparedness, digital health, innovation, access to affordable medicines, and resilient health systems. It will also provide an important platform for advancing the health priorities of the Global South and promoting collective action towards achieving Universal Health Coverage and the health-related Sustainable Development Goals.

headline: 332 Sanitation Worker Deaths Reported in 18 States, UTs Due to Hazardous Sewer and Septic Tank Cleaning Between January 2021 and June 2026
fullText:
Ministry of Social Justice & Empowerment

332 Sanitation Worker Deaths Reported in 18 States, UTs Due to Hazardous Sewer and Septic Tank Cleaning Between January 2021 and June 2026

Government Reiterates Commitment to Complete Eradication of Manual Scavenging: Minister of State for Social Justice and Empowerment Shri Ramdas Athawale in Written Reply in Lok Sabha

193 FIRs Registered in Last Five Years Against Agencies and Officials for Hazardous Manual Cleaning

21 JUL 2026 7:08PM by PIB Delhi

The Government of India has reaffirmed its commitment to the complete eradication of manual scavenging through strict enforcement of the Prohibition of Employment as Manual Scavengers and their Rehabilitation Act, 2013 (MS Act, 2013), rehabilitation of affected persons and promotion of mechanised sanitation systems. The Act, which came into force on 6 December 2013, prohibits the employment or engagement of any person as a manual scavenger and provides stringent penal provisions for violations.

This information was given today by Minister of State for Social Justice and Empowerment Shri Ramdas Athawale in a written reply to Member of Parliament Dr D Ravi Kumar in Lok Sabha.

The House was informed that under Section 8 of the MS Act, 2013, any person or agency found employing or engaging a manual scavenger is liable for punishment with imprisonment for a term extending up to two years, a fine of up to ₹1 lakh, or both, reinforcing the Government's zero-tolerance approach towards the inhuman practice.

The written reply further stated that, as per information received from the National Commission for Safai Karamcharis (NCSK), 332 sanitation workers lost their lives in 18 States and Union Territories, while undertaking hazardous cleaning of sewers and septic tanks during the period from 1 January 2021 to 30 June 2026.

As per the written reply, 193 First Information Reports (FIRs) have been registered during the last five years by the concerned States and Union Territories against agencies and officials responsible for hazardous manual cleaning, in accordance with the provisions of the law.

Replying to supplementary question, the House was informed that no separate evaluation has been undertaken regarding the implementation of mechanised sewer cleaning across States and Union Territories.

The House was also informed that recent surveys conducted across all districts have not reported any instance of the practice of manual scavenging, indicating the positive impact of sustained legislative, administrative and rehabilitation efforts undertaken by the Government to eliminate the practice.

The Government continues to strengthen measures for the safety, dignity and welfare of sanitation workers through strict enforcement of the law, promotion of mechanised sanitation technologies and comprehensive rehabilitation initiatives, reaffirming its commitment to achieving a manual scavenging-free India.

(Release ID: 2287317)
