headline: FUNDS FOR DAY-NRLM
fullText:
Ministry of Rural Development

FUNDS FOR DAY-NRLM

21 JUL 2026 7:06PM by PIB Delhi

The total budget allocation, funds sanctioned, and expenditure incurred under the Deendayal Antyodaya Yojana – National Rural Livelihoods Mission (DAY-NRLM), State-wise including Tamil Nadu during FY 2026-27 are given at Annexure-I.

Under Deendayal Antyodaya Yojana – National Rural Livelihoods Mission (DAY-NRLM), 10.05 crore rural households have been organised into 92 lakh SHGs in the country.

Under the Deendayal Antyodaya Yojana–National Rural Livelihoods Mission (DAY-NRLM), the Ministry of Rural Development has adopted a multipronged strategy for the social, economic, and institutional empowerment of rural women. The positive outcomes of these interventions have been validated through independent impact evaluation studies commissioned by the Ministry. The 3ie Impact Evaluation Study (2025), based on two rounds of data collection conducted in 2019 and 2024 across 9 States, covering about 23,000 households, found that household incomes increased by 9.5% in NRLM blocks, while income gains in mature community institution clusters ranged from 12% to 33%. The study also reported an increase of 6–11% in per capita household consumption, along with improvements in women’s empowerment, livelihood diversification, and overall household welfare.

Further, NITI Aayog’s Evaluation of Centrally Sponsored Schemes in the Rural Development Sector (2025) assessed DAY-NRLM as a highly relevant and impactful programme, noting its contribution to increased incomes, diversified livelihood opportunities, enhanced financial inclusion and literacy, greater self-confidence among women, and improved social inclusion through strong community institutions and convergence with other development initiatives. These findings underscore the significant role of DAY-NRLM in advancing women’s empowerment and improving the socio-economic well-being of rural households.

The Ministry has put in place a robust mechanism to ensure timely fund release, capacity building, and technical support for SHGs and community institutions under DAY-NRLM. Funds are released to States/UTs in a timely manner based on approved allocations and utilization requirements. To strengthen financial management, expenditure monitoring, and transparency, the Ministry has developed the e-Financial Management and Accounting System (e-FMAS/GramNidhi), which is integrated with LokOS and the Public Financial Management System (PFMS) for real-time tracking and efficient fund flow.

Capacity building is a core strategy of DAY-NRLM aimed at strengthening the knowledge, skills, leadership, and institutional capacities of SHGs, their federations, community cadres, and mission staff. Experienced SHG members are nurtured as Community Resource Persons (CRPs) and para-professionals to provide local mentoring and technical guidance, while 619 Community Managed Training Centres (CMTCs) function as decentralized training hubs at the block level. Capacity-building interventions cover institution building, financial management, livelihoods, governance, social inclusion, digital literacy, and entrepreneurship. The Mission also leverages digital platforms such as LokOS for data-driven monitoring, performance tracking, and informed decision-making, thereby strengthening the effectiveness and sustainability of SHGs and community institutions.

The implementation of DAY-NRLM is carried out through a well-defined institutional architecture supported by regular capacity-building, continuous handholding, robust monitoring mechanisms, and digital platforms for fund flow and programme management. The Mission places significant emphasis on awareness generation, training of SHG members and community cadres, and strengthening of community institutions through Community Resource Persons (CRPs), Community Managed Training Centres (CMTCs), and dedicated support structures at the National, State, District, and Block levels. To ensure smooth implementation, the Ministry continuously reviews programme progress with States/UTs, provides technical guidance and capacity-building support, and leverages digital platforms such as LokOS for effective monitoring and transparency.

The Ministry of Rural Development has undertaken several initiatives to strengthen the branding and marketing ecosystem for products made by Self-Help Group (SHG) members, including promotion of the Saras, Saras Aajeevika and Aajeevika brands and development of State-level brands. Every year, the Ministry organizes two National-level Saras Melas, one Saras Food Festival, and more than 50 State-level Saras Melas and Food Festivals, providing SHG members with opportunities to showcase and sell their products in urban markets. The Ministry also operates the Saras Aajeevika Gallery in New Delhi and has established the eSaras e-commerce platform, besides partnering with GeM, ONDC, Amazon, Flipkart, Meesho and JioMart to enhance market access and sales opportunities for SHG products.

Further, to strengthen sustainable livelihoods under DAY-NRLM, regular training and capacity-building programmes are conducted for SHG members and community cadres on financial literacy, livelihood promotion and enterprise development. In the farm sector, women are supported through agro-ecological farming, livestock-based livelihoods, Integrated Farming Clusters and dedicated Community Resource Persons such as Krishi Sakhi, Pashu Sakhi, Van Sakhi and Matshya Sakhi and in Non farm sector Start-up Village Entrepreneurship Programme (SVEP) supports rural non-farm enterprises.

Under Deendayal Antyodaya Yojana - National Rural Livelihoods Mission (DAY-NRLM), this Ministry implements Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Grameen Kaushalya Yojana (DDU-GKY) and Rural Self Employment Training Institutes (RSETI) programmes for skill development of rural poor youth through their employment to eradicate poverty in the country.

This information was given by the Minister of State for Rural Development DR. CHANDRA SEKHAR PEMMASANI in a written reply in Lok Sabha today.

headline: PHASE-WISE CONSTRUCTION OF RURAL ROADS UNDER PMGSY
fullText:
Ministry of Rural Development

PHASE-WISE CONSTRUCTION OF RURAL ROADS UNDER PMGSY

21 JUL 2026 7:04PM by PIB Delhi

The details of total length of roads constructed and number of habitations connected under all verticals of Pradhan Mantri Gram Sadak Yojana till 16.07.2026 since inception, including for the States of Jharkhand, Gujarat and Rajasthan, and specifically for Chatra, Pali and Dahod Lok Sabha Constituencies, are given at Annexure-I.

Further, the phase-wise, state-wise and district-wise physical details of road length sanctioned, completed, under-construction, and habitations connected till date are available in the public domain via the Online Management, Monitoring and Accounting System (OMMAS) portal and may be seen at https://pmgsy.dord.gov.in/dbweb.

The details of roads sanctioned, completed, under construction and proposed under PMGSY along with the funds sanctioned, released and utilized during the last three years and the current year (as on 16.07.2026), year-wise and state-wise are given at Annexure-II.

Further, the details of roads sanctioned, completed, under construction and proposed under PMGSY along with funds utilized during the last three years and the current year, year-wise, State-wise and district-wise are available in the public domain via the Online Management, Monitoring and Accounting System (OMMAS) portal and may be seen at https://pmgsy.dord.gov.in/dbweb.

Further, year-wise details of roads sanctioned, completed, under construction and proposed under PMGSY, along with funds utilized during the last three years and the current year for Chatra Lok Sabha Constituency, are given at Annexure-III.

For the Financial Year 2026-27, physical targets for the construction and upgradation of rural roads under PMGSY are finalized in consultation with the respective State Governments during the Annual Action Plan (AAP) meetings, based on the balance of sanctioned works and the availability of budgetary resources. District-wise targets are subsequently allocated by the States/UTs within the state-wise targets allocated by the Ministry of Rural Development. The FY 2026-27 physical targets for various PMGSY verticals, including Pradhan Mantri Janjati Adivasi Nyaya Maha Abhiyan (PM-JANMAN) for connectivity to Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Groups (PVTGs) areas, are given at Annexure-IV.

PMGSY roads are constructed by the State Governments with a design life of at least 10 years. As per PMGSY guidelines, maintenance of roads constructed under the programme is the responsibility of the State Governments. All PMGSY road works are covered by an initial five year maintenance contracts to be entered into along with the construction contract, with the same contractor, as per the Standard Bidding Document. Maintenance funds to service the contract are required to be budgeted by the State Governments and placed at the disposal of the State Rural Roads Development Agencies (SRRDAs) in a separate maintenance account. On expiry of this 5 year post construction maintenance, PMGSY roads are required to be placed under Zonal maintenance contracts consisting of 5 year maintenance including renewal as per cycle, from time to time.

The program guidelines of Pradhan Mantri Gram Sadak Yojana (PMGSY) incorporate dedicated relaxation parameters to ensure prioritized connectivity for special category areas, including Aspirational Districts, Tribal (Schedule-V) areas, Desert Areas, and Special Category areas. While eligibility criteria for other habitations for getting connectivity under the scheme is a population of 500 persons (as per 2001 census under PMGSY-I and as per Census 2011 under PMGSY-IV), for Tribal (Schedule V) areas and Selected Tribal and Backward Districts (as identified by the Ministry of Home Affairs and Planning Commission) and for North-Eastern and Himalayan States, unconnected habitations with a population of 250 and above are eligible for providing all-weather road connectivity under the scheme.

Furthermore, under the recently launched PMGSY-IV, these relaxed population criteria (250+) continue to apply to Aspirational Districts/Blocks, Tribal Schedule-V areas, and Desert Areas based on Census 2011 data, with a further relaxation down to a population of 100 or more in districts affected by Left Wing Extremism.

Further, the Government has also integrated the road connectivity components of the Dharti Aaba Janjatiya Gram Utkarsh Abhiyan (DA-JGUA) and the Pradhan Mantri Anusuchit Jaati Abhyuday Yojana (PM-AJAY) through convergence with PMGSY-IV. This convergence framework applies to all States and Union Territories. Under this framework priority is given to unconnected habitations identified under DA-JGUA that have a population of 500 or more with at least 50% Scheduled Tribe population, or those in Aspirational Districts with a population of 250 or more including at least 50 Scheduled Tribe members. Similarly, under PM-AJAY, priority is extended to unconnected habitations with a population of 500 or more and at least 40% Scheduled Caste population as per Census 2011.

In addition to the above, road connectivity component of Pradhan Mantri Janjati Adivasi Nyaya Maha Abhiyan (PM-JANMAN) is also being implemented as a separate vertical under PMGSY to provide connectivity to the habitations of Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Groups(PVTG).

Under these systematic guidelines, eligible unconnected habitations falling within the tribal-dominated districts of Dahod (Gujarat), Palghar (Maharashtra), Kalahandi, and Nuapada (Odisha) are extended connectivity benefits under the relaxed norms. Project sanctions for these areas are processed based on the core network planning and Detailed Project Reports (DPRs) formulated and submitted by the respective State Governments in compliance with the programme frameworks.

The PMGSY was launched as 100% Centrally Sponsored Scheme. However, subsequently, on the basis of the recommendations of the Sub-Group of Chief Ministers on Rationalization of Centrally Sponsored Schemes the fund sharing pattern of PMGSY was changed in the ratio of 60:40 between the Centre and States for all States/UT including Gujarat except for 8 North Eastern and 3 Himalayan States/UTs (with legislature) for which it is 90:10 with effect from 2015-16. The Central share for the UTs without legislature is 100%.

This information was given by the Minister of State for Rural Development SHRI KAMLESH PASWAN in a written reply in Lok Sabha today.

summaryText:
### OVERVIEW
The Minister of State for Rural Development delivered a written reply in Lok Sabha on 21 July 2026, providing phase‑wise construction targets and status details for Pradhan Mantri Gram Sadak Yojana (PMGSY) roads up to 16 July 2026, including fund sharing ratios, eligibility norms, maintenance provisions, and convergence with other schemes.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS
- Reply date: 21 July 2026; data reflect status as of 16 July 2026.  
- FY 2026‑27 physical construction and upgradation targets for PMGSY have been finalized in consultation with respective State Governments during Annual Action Plan meetings.  
- Road works are implemented by State Governments with a minimum design life of 10 years.  
- Maintenance structure: a five‑year post‑construction contract with the original contractor, followed by zonal five‑year maintenance contracts after expiry.  
- Fund sharing pattern: 60 % Centre : 40 % State for all states/UTs, except 8 North‑Eastern and 3 Himalayan states/UTs (90 % Centre : 10 % State) and Union Territories without legislature (100 % Centre), effective from FY 2015‑16.  
- Eligibility for road connectivity: 500 persons for general habitations; 250 + persons for tribal (Schedule‑V) areas, selected tribal/backward districts, and NE/Himalayan states, with further relaxation to 100 + persons in districts affected by Left Wing Extremism.  
- Convergence with DA‑JGUA and PM‑AJAY: priority given to habitations meeting specific population and Scheduled Tribe/Scheduled Caste percentage criteria.  
- PM‑JANMAN vertical provides road connectivity to Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Group habitations, with project sanctions based on State‑submitted Detailed Project Reports.  
- Eligible habitations in tribal‑dominated districts of Dahod (Gujarat), Palghar (Maharashtra), Kalahandi and Nuapada (Odisha) are covered under the relaxed norms.  
- Detailed state‑wise, district‑wise, and vertical‑wise data on sanctioned, completed, under‑construction, and proposed road lengths and fund utilization are publicly available on the OMMAS portal (https://pmgsy.dord.gov.in/dbweb).

headline: ACHIEVEMENT OF TARGET UNDER PMAY-G
fullText:
Ministry of Rural Development

ACHIEVEMENT OF TARGET UNDER PMAY-G

21 JUL 2026 7:01PM by PIB Delhi

The State/UT-wise details of number of houses sanctioned, houses completed and houses under construction against the target year under PMAY-G during the last 5 years i.e. 2021-22 to 2025-26 is provided in Annexure-I.

State/UT-wise cumulative progress of houses sanctioned and completed as on 16.07.2026 is provided in Annexure-II.

During the last five years (i.e. 2021-22 to 2025-26), a total Central share of ₹ 1,34,242.45 crore has been released to the States/UTs under PMAY-G. This includes ₹ 19,013.72 crore released to Madhya Pradesh and ₹14,898.43 crore released to Maharashtra by the Ministry. The State/UT-wise details of financial assistance released under the scheme during the last five years, including the approved financial allocation for the year 2026-27, are provided in Annexure-III.

A number of steps have been taken by the Government to accelerate construction of houses in all States/UTs, to achieve the targets under PMAY-G. These include:

(i) Timely allocation of targets to the States/UTs and release of adequate funds;

(ii) Regular review of progress;

(iii) Monitoring and supervision of the scheme on a Dashboard;

(iv) Regular Workshops are conducted by the Ministry with the States/UTs;

(v) Rural Mason Training program, etc.

In addition to the unit assistance provided under the scheme, financial assistance of ₹12,000 is extendaed for the construction of toilets through convergence with the Swachh Bharat Mission–Gramin(SBM-G).Further, there is a provision for providing prescribed person-days of unskilled wage employment within the approved scheme parameters, at prevailing wage rates to each PMAY-G beneficiary for the construction of their house through convergence with MGNREGS/ VB G-RAM G.

PMAY-G households are also provided with piped drinking water through convergence with the Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) and Har Ghar Nal Se Jal (HGNSJ) of the Department of Drinking Water and Sanitation, Ministry of Jal Shakti, or other similar schemes. In addition, beneficiaries are supported with electricity connections through relevant schemes implemented by the Ministry of Power, State Governments, or UT Administrations, and are provided Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) connections under the Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala Yojana (PMUY) of the Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas or other relevant schemes.

Convergence under PMAY-G also includes the inclusion of women members of beneficiary households in Self-Help Groups (SHGs), thereby promoting their social and economic empowerment.

At the national level, as on 16.07.2026, a total of 3.08 crore houses have been provided with toilets, 2.43 crore with water connections, 3.07 crore with electricity connections, 2.9 crore with LPG connections, and 1.35 crore beneficiary households have been associated with Self-Help Groups (SHGs).

A number of assessments have been undertaken by agencies like the National Institute of Public Finance and Policy (NIPFP), National Institute of Rural Development & Panchayati Raj (NIRD&PR), National Institution for Transforming India (NITI Aayog) and Indian Institute of Management- Ahmedabad (IIM-A) regarding the impact of PMAY-G, the latest study being by IIM-A. All the studies have reported a positive impact on housing and quality of life in general.

The Ministry had engaged IIM-A to carry out an independent evaluation of PMAY-G across 12 States/UTs. The study (2026) found that PMAY-G has significantly improved the living standards of rural households through enhanced housing security, better sanitation, improved health outcomes, higher household income and savings, reduction in houselessness and distress migration, and greater financial inclusion. The evaluation also observed positive impacts on beneficiaries’ dignity, quality of life, and overall socio-economic well-being.

This information was given by the Minister of State for Rural Development DR. CHANDRA SEKHAR PEMMASANI in a written reply in Lok Sabha today.

headline: REVISION OF AMOUNT UNDER NSAP
fullText:
Ministry of Rural Development

REVISION OF AMOUNT UNDER NSAP

21 JUL 2026 6:58PM by PIB Delhi

National Social Assistance Programme (NSAP) is being implemented across the country as a social security programme, for vulnerable sections of the society such as old aged, widows and disabled persons belonging to Below Poverty Line (BPL). The rate of Central assistance under NSAP pension schemes including National Old Age Pension Scheme, National Widow Pension Scheme and National Disability Pension Scheme was last revised in 2012.

NSAP caters to more than 3 crore BPL beneficiaries and as per the information collected from States/UTs, about 5.9 crore additional pensioners are supported by state-funded social pension schemes. Therefore, around 9 crore beneficiaries are receiving pension benefits either under Central pension schemes and/or under State-specific social security pension schemes, many of which have expanded coverage and higher assistance levels tailored to local needs. At present, States are giving top up amount to the beneficiaries ranging from ₹50/- to ₹5700/- per month. As a result, NSAP pensioners are getting monthly pension on an average of Rs. 1100/- in several States/ UTs. State/UT-wise details of top-up paid by the States/UTs are at Annexure.

From time to time, various impact assessment and evaluation studies have been carried out to assess the impact of NSAP schemes by the Ministry and NITI Aayog. The assessments indicate satisfactory implementation at the grassroots level, with most beneficiaries expressing overall satisfaction with the processes of selection, sanction, and disbursement of pensions. The studies also find that pension support has helped beneficiaries meet essential expenses such as food, healthcare, etc., thereby reducing their dependence on family members for these needs. At the same time, they have recommended further strengthening of the programme through measures such as improving adequacy of assistance, ensuring timely and regular payments, enhancing beneficiary verification and monitoring systems etc.

At present, there is no proposal under consideration to revise the rate of assistance under NSAP.

This information was given by the Minister of State for Rural Development SHRI KAMLESH PASWAN in a written reply in Lok Sabha today.

(Release ID: 2287305)
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW
The Ministry of Rural Development has clarified that there is no proposal to revise the rate of assistance under the National Social Assistance Programme (NSAP), despite the programme being last revised in 2012.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS
* The National Social Assistance Programme (NSAP) is a social security programme for vulnerable sections of society such as the old-aged, widows, and disabled persons belonging to Below Poverty Line (BPL).
* Around 9 crore beneficiaries are receiving pension benefits either under Central pension schemes and/or under State-specific social security pension schemes.
* States/UTs are providing a top-up amount ranging from ₹50 to ₹5700 per month, resulting in an average monthly pension of ₹1100 in several States/UTs.
* Impact assessment and evaluation studies have been carried out by the Ministry and NITI Aayog, indicating satisfactory implementation at the grassroots level and overall satisfaction among beneficiaries.
* The studies recommend further strengthening of the programme through measures such as ensuring timely and regular payments, enhancing beneficiary verification and monitoring systems, etc.
* There is no proposal under consideration to revise the rate of assistance under NSAP.

headline: Fertiliser, seeds and monsoon, and El Niño situation reviewed in weekly review meeting of Agriculture Ministry
fullText:
Ministry of Agriculture &

Fertiliser, seeds and monsoon, and El Niño situation reviewed in weekly review meeting of Agriculture Ministry

South-West Monsoon covers entire country, favourable conditions created for sowing of Kharif crops

Union Agriculture Minister Shri Shivraj Singh Chouhan gives instructions for continuous monitoring of agricultural activities in sensitive districts

Shri Shivraj Chouhan asks to ensure timely availability of fertilisers to farmers and quick resolution of any shortage in distribution system

21 JUL 2026 6:33PM by PIB Delhi

Under the chairmanship of Union Minister for Agriculture and Farmers’ Welfare, and Rural Development, Shri Shivraj Singh Chouhan, a detailed review was done in New Delhi today to assess the progress of sowing of Kharif crops, the impact of El Niño and current situation of monsoon, situation of sensitive districts with deficient rainfall, availability of fertilisers and seeds, water storage in reservoirs, etc.

Union Agriculture Minister Shri Shivraj Singh Chouhan directed officials to keep special monitoring on areas with deficient rainfall and maintain continuous coordination with the states. It was informed during the meeting that till July 17, 2026, the total sowing area of Kharif crops in the country has been recorded at 658.19 lakh hectares. Although there has been a decrease in the total sowing area compared to last year, positive progress has been seen in sowing under paddy, pulses and other crops in many states. Shri Shivraj Chouhan directed that weekly review meetings should be organised by pulses, oilseeds and cotton missions with state governments and quick action should be ensured based on feedback received from field level.

In the meeting, the forecasts of the India Meteorological Department (IMD), possible El Niño conditions and the situation of 270 sensitive districts across the country from the point of view of rainfall were reviewed. During this, a detailed presentation was given on the situation of monsoon and rainfall. The situation of rainfall in various states, areas with less and more rainfall, progress of sowing of crops and the condition of crops based on indicators like NDVI (Normalised Difference Vegetation Index) were also reviewed. In the meeting, while reviewing the progress of monsoon, discussions were also held on favourable conditions for agricultural activities during the Kharif season.

It was informed in the meeting that the South-West Monsoon spread completely across the country by July 9, 2026, covering the remaining parts of Rajasthan, Haryana and Punjab. Generally, the monsoon covers the entire country by July 8, while this year its expansion was completed only one day after the normal date. With the nationwide expansion of the monsoon, favourable conditions have been created for the sowing of Kharif crops in most of the states.

During the period from June 1 to July 15, 2026, the average rainfall in the country was recorded at 23 per cent below normal. During this period, 227 mm rainfall occurred across the country, while normal rainfall is 294.2 mm. The highest rainfall deficiency was in the East and Northeast India, where 348 mm rainfall occurred against a normal of 540.2 mm, which means, a deficiency of 36 per cent was recorded. There was a 26 per cent deficiency in Southern Peninsular India, 19 per cent in Northwest India and 13 per cent in Central India. On the basis of these figures, continuous monitoring is being kept on the situation of rainfall and appropriate agricultural management and contingency measures are being ensured in coordination with the states as per requirement. Shri Chouhan directed to maintain continuous monitoring on the situation of rainfall and ensure appropriate agricultural management and contingency measures in coordination with the states as per requirement.

The availability and distribution of fertilisers, especially urea, and the availability of seeds were also reviewed during the meeting. It was informed that adequate availability of fertilisers and seeds has been ensured for Kharif 2026 and continuous monitoring of supply is being done through regular coordination with the states. The Union Minister directed that information on the actual situation should be obtained through Krishi Vigyan Kendras (KVKs) and state governments. He said that it should be ensured that farmers get fertilisers according to their requirement and if there is any shortage in the distribution system, its quick solution should be done.

At the end of the review meeting, Shri Shivraj Singh Chouhan directed both departments of the Agriculture Ministry that continuous monitoring of agricultural activities during the Kharif season should be done through coordination with the states, scientific advice, weather-based agricultural management and effective implementation of schemes to the farmers. He said that the Central Government is fully committed to protecting the interests of farmers and increasing agricultural production.

(Release ID: 2287282)

Read this release in:
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW  
The Union Ministry of Agriculture & Fertilizer reviewed the progress of Kharif crop sowing, monsoon conditions, and agricultural preparedness under El Niño scenarios during a meeting chaired by Union Agriculture Minister Shri Shivraj Singh Chouhan on July 21, 2026.  

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
- **Sowing progress**: Total Kharif crop sowing area recorded at 658.19 lakh hectares by July 17, 2026, with reduced total area compared to previous year but gains in paddy, pulses, and other crops in multiple states.  
- **Monsoon status**: South-West Monsoon fully covered the country by July 9, 2026 (one day later than normal), creating favorable conditions for Kharif sowing.  
- **Rainfall deficits**: Average rainfall from June 1 to July 15, 2026, was 23% below normal (227 mm vs. 294.2 mm expected), with severe deficits in East and Northeast India (36% below normal) and Southern Peninsular India (26% below normal).  
- **Sensitive districts**: Monitoring is ongoing in 270 districts with deficient rainfall, requiring coordinated action with states.  
- **Input availability**: Fertilizers (especially urea) and seeds availability confirmed adequate for Kharif 2026, with continuous supply monitoring via coordination with states and Krishi Vigyan Kendras (KVKs).  
- **Ministerial directives**: Emphasis on weekly review meetings for pulses, oilseeds, and cotton missions; continuous scientific weather-based agricultural management; and swift resolution of fertilizer distribution shortages.

headline: FUNCTIONING OF PANCHAYATI RAJ INSTITUTIONS
fullText:
Ministry of Panchayati Raj

FUNCTIONING OF PANCHAYATI RAJ INSTITUTIONS

21 JUL 2026 6:18PM by PIB Delhi

“Panchayat”, being “Local Government”, is a State subject and part of State List of Seventh Schedule of the Constitution of India. Panchayats are set up and operate through the respective State Panchayati Raj Acts. States have made their own Panchayati Raj Act, subject to the provisions of the Constitution. Article 243G of the Constitution empowers the Legislature of a State to make provisions, by law, for the devolution of power and responsibilities upon Panchayat at the appropriate level, subject to such conditions as may be specified, with respect to the preparation of plans for economic development and social justice and implementation of schemes for economic development and social justice as may be entrusted to them including those in relation to matters listed in the Eleventh Schedule to the Constitution. The State legislatures are to consider the 29 subjects listed in the Eleventh Schedule for devolution of power and responsibilities to Panchayats. Accordingly, all matters relating to Panchayats, including, to review the functioning of Panchayati Raj Institutions, to devolve greater administrative, financial and functional powers to Gram Panchayats, details of the funds, functions and functionaries devolved to Panchayats and to take steps to strengthen local self-Governance and ensure greater autonomy, accountability and capacity of Panchayati Raj Institutions, fall within the purview of State Government.

However, the Ministry of Panchayati Raj reviews performance of Panchayats, from time to time, through studies, review meetings, field visits, video conferencing, Information Technology applications etc. Year-wise details of the funds, functions and functionaries devolved to Panchayats by States are not maintained by Ministry of Panchayati Raj.

The Ministry has released a report titled “Status of Devolution to Panchayats in States - An Indicative Evidence-Based Ranking, 2024” in February 2025 to assess the effectiveness of devolution and the role of local governments in strengthening grassroots democracy. This report presents the Devolution Index, which provides the overall scores and ranks for all States/Union Territories (UTs) covered under Part-IX of the Constitution, based on six identified dimensions: Framework, Functions, Finances, Functionaries, Capacity Enhancement, and Accountability. This report reveals that the extent of devolution has increased from 39.9% to 43.9% between the period 2013-14 to 2021-22. Ranking of States/UTs in Devolution Index and Sub-indices is attached as Annexure-I.

In these six identified dimensions, Framework includes reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes/ Scheduled Tribes and women, Panchayats elections & State Election Commission, Panchayats duration, dissolution & bye elections, constitution and function of District Planning Committee, role of Panchayats in Committees and parallel bodies/institutions, autonomy to Panchayats. Functions include functions assigned to Panchayats including Activity Mapping, expenditure incurred and actual involvement of Panchayats, involvement of Panchayats in important Schemes. Finances include Fifteenth Finance Commission Grants to the Panchayats, State Finance Commission (SFC), money transfers to Panchayat on accounts of the SFC recommendations, empowerment of Panchayats to impose and collect revenue, funds available with Panchayats, expenditure of Panchayats, initiatives related to finance, accounts and budget. Functionaries include physical infrastructure of Panchayats, e-connectivity of Panchayats, Panchayats officials. Capacity Enhancement includes training institutions, training activities–training of elected representative and officials. Accountability includes accounting and audit of Panchayats, social audit of Panchayats, functioning of Gram Sabha, transparency & anti-corruption, Panchayats assessment & incentivization.

To strengthen local self-Governance and ensure greater autonomy, accountability and capacity of Panchayati Raj Institutions, the Ministry has implemented a Centrally Sponsored Scheme, namely the Revamped Rashtriya Gram Swaraj Abhiyan (RGSA), w.e.f. financial year 2022-23, with the objective of supporting Panchayati Raj institutions by imparting trainings to Elected Representatives and other stakeholders to develop leadership abilities, thereby enabling Gram Panchayats to function effectively across all States/UTs. Under the scheme, capacity building support is provided to elected representatives, functionaries, and other stakeholders of Panchayats under different categories, viz. basic orientation and refresher programmes, thematic interventions, specialized trainings and Panchayat Development Plan-related programmes, etc. The scheme also supports exposure visits to facilitate experiential learning and replication of best practices for strengthening Panchayat governance, as well as development of training modules and materials etc.

Further, to enhance skills and knowledge, a major step has also been taken to build capacity and train elected representatives and functionaries of Panchayats through Institutes of excellence such as IIMs/IITs under the Leadership/Management Development Program (MDP).

The Ministry of Panchayati Raj, in collaboration with IIM Ahmedabad, has also developed a training module on Own Source Revenue (OSR) to strengthen the financial self-reliance of Gram Panchayats. The module helps elected representatives and Panchayat functionaries understand how OSR is generated through tax and non-tax sources.

Additionally, the Ministry has launched a Specialized Training Module as a part of “Shashkta Panchayat Netri Abhiyan” for the capacity building of Women Elected Representatives of Panchayati Raj Institutions. The focus of this training module is to build the capacity of Women Elected Representatives across different aspects of rural governance and enhance their knowledge and practical skills for the effective discharge of roles and responsibilities as elected representatives, thereby promoting women-led governance.

General Category States

headline: E-GRAM SWARAJ PORTAL IN SCHEDULED TRIBE-DOMINATED AREAS
fullText:
Ministry of Panchayati Raj

E-GRAM SWARAJ PORTAL IN SCHEDULED TRIBE-DOMINATED AREAS

21 JUL 2026 6:17PM by PIB Delhi

The Parliament enacted the Provisions of the Panchayats (Extension to the Scheduled Areas) Act, 1996 (PESA) under Article 243M(4)(b) of the Constitution to extend the provisions of Part IX of the Constitution to the Fifth Schedule Areas with certain exceptions and modifications. Panchayats, being "Local Government", are a State subject and operate through the respective State Panchayati Raj Acts. Accordingly, implementation of PESA, including the adoption and utilisation of digital governance applications such as eGramSwaraj and AuditOnline in Scheduled Areas, falls within the purview of the respective State Governments.

The Ministry of Panchayati Raj facilitates digital governance through the e-Panchayat Mission Mode Project (MMP), including eGramSwaraj and AuditOnline, and periodically reviews their implementation with the States through review meetings and other monitoring mechanisms. The Ministry has also developed 10 PESA Indicators to facilitate assessment of PESA implementation by the States, one of which relates to the uploading of Gram Panchayat Development Plans (GPDPs) of PESA Gram Sabhas on the eGramSwaraj portal.

The State-wise details of utilisation of eGramSwaraj/ status of GPDP uploads in PESA areas, are placed below.

S.No. | State Name | Total PESA Panchayat | Total Villages | Approved PESA Main Plan

1 | Andhra Pradesh | 609 | 1613 | 609

2 | Chhattisgarh | 5552 | 9891 | 5532

3 | Gujarat | 2889 | 4701 | 2755

4 | Himachal Pradesh | 178 | 1533 | 164

5 | Jharkhand | 2066 | 17240 | 2066

6 | Madhya Pradesh | 5197 | 7710 | 5173

7 | Maharashtra | 2918 | 4317 | 2907

8 | Odisha | 1390 | 11668 | 1390

9 | Rajasthan | 1608 | 5690 | 1604

10 | Telangana | 1314 | 1321 | 1314

Total | 23,721 | 65,684 | 23,514

The Ministry of Panchayati Raj has initiated Gram Panchayat Development Plan (GPDP) integration of PESA within the GPDP framework to enable the preparation of PESA-specific Village Development Plans. A dedicated PESA-GPDP Portal has been launched by the Ministry to facilitate the preparation, implementation, and monitoring of Gram Panchayat Development Plans (GPDPs) in Scheduled Areas under the provisions of the PESA Act.

Further, in Plan Year 2026-27, PESA has been included as a separate theme in eGramSwaraj Portal with 42 activities. It has also been made mandatory for Gram Sabhas in Fifth Schedule Areas to take up at least one activity under PESA theme while preparing their GPDP plan.

In certain rural and tribal areas, challenges such as inadequate digital infrastructure, intermittent internet connectivity, and limited technical capacity have been experienced, affecting the adoption of digital governance applications. To address these challenges, the Ministry of Panchayati Raj, in coordination with the State Governments, provides continuous technical support, undertakes periodic reviews of the implementation of the e-Panchayat applications, and upgrades the applications based on feedback received from the stakeholders. Further, internet connectivity to Gram Panchayats is being strengthened through the BharatNet project being implemented by the Department of Telecommunications to provide broadband connectivity to Gram Panchayats across the country.

Panchayat, being “Local Self Government”, is a State subject and part of the State list of the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution of India. However, the Ministry of Panchayati Raj is implementing the Centrally Sponsored Scheme of revamped Rashtriya Gram Swaraj Abhiyan (RGSA) since the financial year 2022-23 in States/ UTs, including tribal/PESA areas with the objectives to strengthen the capacity of Panchayati Raj institutions (PRIs) by imparting training to Elected Representatives (ERs), Panchayat Functionaries and other stakeholders to develop their governance capabilities for leadership roles, enabling the Gram Panchayats to function effectively across all States/UTs.

Under the scheme, the Ministry provides support for Capacity Building & Training (CB&T) of Panchayat representatives and Officials in different categories, including various digital trainings as per the priority of the States/UTs. Since, 2022-23 to 2026-27 (as on 10.07.2026), 1,87,238 Panchayat representatives and officials have been trained in PESA States to strengthen digital governance and other digital platforms.

This information was given by Union Minister Shri Rajiv Ranjan Singh alias Lalan Singh in a written reply in Lok Sabha on 21st July 2026.

(Release ID: 2287259)

Read this release in:
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW  
The Ministry of Panchayati Raj has launched initiatives to strengthen digital governance in Scheduled Tribal areas by integrating the Panchayats (Extension to the Scheduled Areas) Act, 1996 (PESA) with the eGramSwaraj platform and Gram Panchayat Development Plans (GPDPs), while addressing challenges like digital infrastructure gaps through technical support and connectivity projects.  

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
- The Provisions of PESA, 1996, have been extended to Fifth Schedule Areas, with digital tools like eGramSwaraj and e-Panchayat Mission Mode Project (MMP) adoption monitored by State Governments.  
- A dedicated PESA-GPDP Portal has been launched to streamline preparation, implementation, and monitoring of Gram Panchayat Development Plans in Scheduled Areas.  
- For Plan Year 2026-27, PESA is included as a separate theme in the eGramSwaraj Portal with 42 activities, and Gram Sabhas in Fifth Schedule Areas must include at least one PESA activity in their GPDP plans.  
- As of July 2026, 23,721 PESA Gram Panchayats across 15 states manage 65,684 villages, with 23,514 having approved main plans.  
- Since 2022-23, 1,87,238 Panchayat representatives and officials in PESA states have received training to enhance digital governance capabilities.  
- Challenges such as inadequate digital infrastructure and intermittent connectivity are addressed through technical support, periodic reviews, application upgrades, and the BharatNet project for broadband expansion to Gram Panchayats.

headline: SVAMITVA SCHEME IN UTTAR PRADESH
fullText:
Ministry of Panchayati Raj

SVAMITVA SCHEME IN UTTAR PRADESH

21 JUL 2026 6:16PM by PIB Delhi

As on 13th July 2026, drone survey has been completed in all the notified 90573 villages in Uttar Pradesh and 1.01 Crore property cards have been distributed across nearly 67 thousand villages in the State.

As on 13th July, 2026, about 1.20 crore Property Cards have been prepared covering nearly 75 thousand villages, which is about 83% of the notified villages in the State of Uttar Pradesh. The SVAMITVA Scheme has been extended till September 2026.

The SVAMITVA Scheme aims to provide Record of Rights to property owners in rural inhabited (abadi) areas through drone-based surveys and Continuously Operating Reference Stations (CORS) technology for accurate mapping and demarcation of properties. The Scheme includes ground verification and resolution of objections/disputes by the competent State authorities before issuance of Property Cards, thereby ensuring transparency and fair demarcation of properties.

This information was given by Union Minister Shri Rajiv Ranjan Singh alias Lalan Singh in a written reply in Lok Sabha on 21st July 2026.

headline: Agricultural Census (2021-22)
fullText:
Ministry of Agriculture &

Agricultural Census (2021-22)

21 JUL 2026 6:05PM by PIB Delhi

Agriculture Census in India is conducted after every five years in collaboration with the States and Union Territories.The field work of Agriculture Census is carried out in three distinct phases. The ongoing 11th Agriculture Census with the reference year 2021-22 has been commenced in 2022 and so far, field work & data collection for three phases have been completed within time frame.

Further, in phase-I, data on primary characteristics of agricultural holdings and area operated are collected on complete enumeration basis. In Phase-II, data on characteristics of holdings like cropping pattern, irrigation status etc. are collected from randomly selected 20% of sample villages in each Tehsil. In phase III, data on input use pattern of operational holdings are collected from randomly selected 7% of sample villages in each Tehsil.

Agriculture Census provides reliable data base on socio-economic aspects of operational holders in the country which is used for making agricultural policies. It provides information on the number, size and distribution of operational holdings by classes (marginal, small, semi-medium, medium and large),tenancy, consolidation of holdings, land distribution etc. which is used for efficient planning for utilization of land, irrigation facilities, crop diversification, mechanization and other agricultural inputs. It provides data on woman-operated land holdings to support gender-based agricultural interventions. Census data also enables Government in efficient allocation of subsidies, credit facilities, targeting rural development programs, and establishing national and regional agricultural priorities. Further, it serves as a key data source in agriculture sector for researchers, planners, and other stakeholders.

This information was given by Minister of State for Agriculture and Farmers Welfare, Shri Ramnath Thakur in a written reply in Lok Sabha today.

(Release ID: 2287251)
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW  
The 11th Agriculture Census (reference year 2021‑22) began in 2022 and has finished its three‑phase fieldwork, delivering comprehensive data on agricultural holdings, land use, and gender distribution for policy and program planning.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS  
- Conducted every five years in partnership with States and Union Territories.  
- Reference year: 2021‑22, Census commenced in 2022.  
- All three phases of fieldwork completed within schedule.  
- **Phase I**: Complete enumeration of primary characteristics of holdings and area operated.  
- **Phase II**: 20 % of villages in each Tehsil sampled for cropping pattern, irrigation status, and other holding characteristics.  
- **Phase III**: 7 % of villages in each Tehsil sampled for input‑use patterns of operational holdings.  
- Data on operational holdings categorized as marginal, small, semi‑medium, medium, and large; includes tenancy, consolidation, and land‑distribution details.  
- Provides specific information on woman‑operated land holdings to support gender‑based interventions.  
- Supplies data to allocate subsidies, credit facilities, target rural development programmes, and set national and regional agricultural priorities.  
- Serves as a primary data source for researchers, planners, and stakeholders.

headline: Financial Assistance for Natural Farming
fullText:
Ministry of Agriculture &

Financial Assistance for Natural Farming

21 JUL 2026 6:05PM by PIB Delhi

Indian Council of Agricultural Research has initiated study from 2020-21 through All India Network Programme on Natural Farming (previously AINP-Organic Farming) with 20 cooperating centres covering 16 States to evaluate 8 major cropping systems and develop package of practices under Natural farming besides evaluation of cropping systems under organic farming. The scheme involves 11 State Agricultural Universities, 8 ICAR institutes/centres and 1 deemed to be university.

Studies on the benefits of natural farming have shown that if natural farming is adopted with the right techniques and in a balanced manner, yields improve. The experiences of many farmers have also proven that improving soil health in the initial years improves crop quality, taste, and long-term yield. Furthermore, reducing chemical costs increases farmers' net profits. Mulching in natural farming helps maintain soil moisture, control weeds, and increase organic matter in the soil. This also increases the activity of soil microorganisms and improves soil health. Multi-cropping increases biodiversity in the field, reduces the incidence of pests and diseases, and improves nutrient utilization. The finding is as below;

Under the network research, crop system-based natural farming packages have been developed for various states, such as groundnut + sesame – fennel + cabbage (Rajasthan and Gujarat), soybean + maize – wheat + mustard (Chhattisgarh and Madhya Pradesh), and cassava (Kerala).

In Bajaura (Himachal Pradesh), Almora (Uttarakhand) and Gangtok (Sikkim), the average system productivity (soybean equivalent) of 6475 kg/ha/year was recorded in the soybean + maize – vegetable pea + coriander (green leaf) crop system (in 5 years) under completely natural farming, which was about 5 percent higher than organic farming/integrated crop management.

In Bhopal and Jabalpur (Madhya Pradesh) and Raipur (Chhattisgarh), the average system productivity (soybean equivalent) of 3346 kg/ha/year was recorded in the soybean + maize – wheat + mustard crop system (in 5 years), which was found to be equivalent to integrated crop management and organic farming.

S.K. In Nagar (Gujarat), Ajmer, and Udaipur (Rajasthan), the average system productivity (cowpea equivalent) of 9209 kg/ha/year was recorded in the cowpea + maize/peanut + sesame – fennel + cauliflower/cabbage cropping system (in 5 years) under complete natural farming. This was found to be comparable to integrated crop management.

Natural farming (NF) management has shown a clear improvement in soil health indicators. Soil organic carbon (SOC) levels increased in natural farming fields over a period of 2–3 years. For example, in trials conducted in the Himalayan regions, SOC increased from approximately 0.90% to 1.15%. The number and diversity index of microorganisms in natural farming soils was found to be higher than in soils cultivated with chemical fertilizers. The abundance and balanced distribution of beneficial bacteria, fungi, and actinomycetes were observed, indicating the development of a healthy soil ecosystem over time. This increase in organic matter and microorganisms in the soil under natural farming improves nutrient cycling and soil structure, laying a strong foundation for long-term soil fertility and crop production sustainability.

Further, NITI Aayog Assessment Report states that approximately 91.2% of farmers adopting natural farming experience, increased crop productivity and significantly improved soil health. 90.1% of farmers reported that adopting natural farming has reduced input costs (production costs).

Under the Mission, there is a provision for an Output Based Incentive of ₹4000/- per acre, per year to a farmer for 2 years (up to 1 acre per farmer). The incentive support is for farmers to practice Natural Farming, to train more farmers, upkeep of livestock, preparation of natural farming inputs including purchase of mixing & storage containers or purchase of inputs from Bio-input Resource Centers (BRCs) etc.

This information was given by Minister of State for Agriculture and Farmers Welfare, Shri Ramnath Thakur in a written reply in Lok Sabha today.

(Release ID: 2287252)
summaryText:
### OVERVIEW
The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare announced an output‑based incentive of ₹4,000 per acre per year for up to two years to support natural farming adoption, alongside ongoing national studies evaluating natural farming packages across multiple states.

### KEY HIGHLIGHTS
- Study launched in 2020‑21 via the All India Network Programme on Natural Farming with 20 cooperating centres covering 16 states; involves 11 State Agricultural Universities, 8 ICAR institutes/centres and 1 deemed university.  
- Crop‑system packages developed for groundnut + sesame – fennel + cabbage (Rajasthan, Gujarat), soybean + maize – wheat + mustard (Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh), cassava (Kerala), etc.  
- Recorded productivity: 6475 kg/ha/year (soybean + maize – vegetable pea + coriander) 5% higher than organic; 3346 kg/ha/year (soybean + maize – wheat + mustard) equivalent to integrated management; 9209 kg/ha/year (cowpea‑based system) comparable to integrated.  
- Soil organic carbon increased from ~0.90% to 1.15% in Himalayan trials over 2‑3 years; microbial diversity and abundance higher.  
- NITI Aayog assessment: 91.2% of adopters report increased productivity; 90.1% report reduced input costs.  
- Output‑Based Incentive provides ₹4000 per acre per year for up to 2 years (maximum 1 acre per farmer) for natural farming practice, training, livestock upkeep, input preparation, and purchases from Bio‑input Resource Centres.  
- Announcement made by Minister of State for Agriculture and Farmers Welfare, Shri Ramnath Thakur, in a written Lok Sabha reply (Release ID: 2287252) dated 21 July 2026.

headline: Initiatives to Strengthen Seed Sector
fullText:
Ministry of Agriculture &

Initiatives to Strengthen Seed Sector

21 JUL 2026 6:04PM by PIB Delhi

Government has taken several measures to strengthen India’s seed sector and enhance its global competitiveness, which include the following:

The Government, through the National Agricultural Research and Education System (NARES), comprising 56 ICAR Institutes, 48 State Agricultural Universities (SAUs), 03 Central Agricultural Universities (CAUs) and 39 centres under the All India Coordinated Research Project (AICRP) on Seed (Crops), is undertaking research and development of improved crop varieties. During the period from 2014 to 2025, a total of 3,236 field crop varieties have been released and notified, of which 2,996 are climate-resilient varieties, including 587 extreme climate-tolerant varieties comprising 341 drought tolerant, 89 flood tolerant, 80 salinity tolerant, 59 heat stress tolerant and 18 cold tolerant varieties.

The crop-wise details are as under:

S. No. | Crop Group | Crop Varieties | Climate-resilient Varieties

1 | Cereals | 1,574 | 1,453

2 | Oilseeds | 444 | 397

3 | Pulses | 473 | 446

4 | Forages | 207 | 185

5 | Fibre | 408 | 392

6 | Sugarcane | 96 | 96

7 | Other Crops | 34 | 27

Total | 3,236 | 2,996

The Government promotes the production and multiplication of breeder, foundation and certified seeds of high-yielding and climate-resilient varieties, including varieties tolerant to drought, heat, flood, salinity and waterlogging, to ensure timely availability of quality seeds suited to diverse agro-climatic conditions across the country.

During the past three years, the Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers’ Rights (PPV&FR) Authority has expedited registration of plant varieties, thereby enabling farmers, ICAR Institutes, State Agricultural Universities and private seed companies to protect the varieties developed by them. So far, 10,802 varieties of 219 crop species have been registered under the PPV&FR Act, out of which 5,689 varieties were registered during 2023–26.

The Government has established an extensive institutional framework for production and distribution of quality seeds. At present, 229 Seed Hubs and 384 Breeder Seed Production Centres are engaged in seed production. Further, 17 State Seed Corporations and National Seeds Corporation Limited, along with national public sector organizations such as Bharatiya Beej SahakariSamiti Limited (BBSSL), Hindustan Insecticides Limited (HIL), National Agricultural Cooperative Marketing Federation of India Ltd. (NAFED), National Fertilizers Limited (NFL), KrishakBharati Cooperative Limited (KRIBHCO), Indian Farm Forestry Development Cooperative Limited (IFFDC) and private seed companies contribute significantly to the production and distribution of quality seeds across the country.

To strengthen quality assurance, 178 notified Seed Testing Laboratories (STLs) are engaged in seed testing across the country. The Government also provides financial assistance for strengthening Seed Testing Laboratories, including support for International Seed Testing Association (ISTA) membership and technical audits for ISTA accreditation.

Seed quality is regulated under the Seeds Act, 1966, the Seeds Rules, 1968 and the Seeds (Control) Order, 1983, as amended from time to time. These statutory provisions empower the State Governments to regulate seed quality, undertake inspections, testing and enforcement, and curb the sale of sub-standard and spurious seeds. To support the regulatory framework, a Seed Authentication, Traceability and Holistic Inventory (SATHI) Portal has been deployed in phased manner to facilitate end-to-end digital traceability in seed production, certification, inventory management and distribution, thereby enhancing transparency and quality assurance across the seed value chain.

To facilitate international trade and improve ease of doing business, the Government has simplified procedures relating to the import and export of seeds and planting material. Following the disbanding of the EXIM Committee in October, 2024, import and export approvals are being facilitated through DGFT’s digital platforms while continuing to ensure compliance with applicable plant quarantine and regulatory requirements. The Government also promotes seed exports through participation in the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) Seed Schemes, International Seed Testing Association (ISTA) framework and National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories (NABL) accreditation of Seed Testing Laboratories to align India’s seed quality systems with internationally accepted standards.

The details of seed imports and exports during the last three years are as under:

Year | Import Qty. (000 MT) | Import Value (₹ crore) | Export Qty. (000 MT) | Export Value (₹ crore)

2024-25 | 27.69 | 1,561.22 | 92.06 | 2,323.39

2025-26 | 31.84 | 1,755.96 | 123.82 | 3,023.16

2026-27 (upto May 2026) | 4.43 | 357.36 | 18.46 | 582.19

India is a founding member of the International Treaty on Plant Genetic Resources for Food and Agriculture (ITPGRFA), which provides a multilateral framework for conservation, sustainable use and exchange of plant genetic resources for food and agriculture and promotes equitable sharing of benefits arising from their use. This provides domestic R&D sector access to global germplasm to help them in developing improved varieties of seeds.

The Government also collaborates with international agricultural research institutions of the CGIAR system, including the International Rice Research Institute (IRRI), International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid Tropics (ICRISAT), International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center (CIMMYT), International Center for Agricultural Research in the Dry Areas (ICARDA) and the Alliance of Bioversity International and the International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT) for exchange of knowledge and identification of suitable climate-resilient seed technologies and best practices. With Government support, the International Rice Research Institute established its South Asia Regional Centre (ISARC) at Varanasi in 2018 for collaborative rice research and capacity building. Further, establishment of the International Potato Center (CIP) South Asia Regional Centre (CSARC) at Singna, Agra, Uttar Pradesh was approved on 25.06.2025.

Government has undertaken several initiatives to promote Public-Private Partnerships (PPP) in the seed sector by facilitating collaboration among ICAR Institutes, State Agricultural Universities (SAUs), State Seed Corporations, National Seeds Corporation (NSC) and private seed companies for seed multiplication, varietal evaluation, breeder-to-certified seed chain management, technology dissemination and licensing of publicly bred varieties and hybrids to private seed companies for large-scale multiplication and distribution, thereby leveraging private sector expertise, investment and outreach.

During the last three years (2023–24 to 2025–26), 1,317 licensing agreements for seeds, crop varieties and planting materials were executed by 27 ICAR Institutes with public and private sector organizations.

Further, the Agriculture Infrastructure Fund (AIF) provides financial support for creation of agriculture infrastructure, including seed processing facilities.

Government also provides financial assistance to States and implementing agencies for seed-related activities such as production and distribution of high-yielding varieties, development of seed infrastructure, seed processing, storage and testing facilities under various programmes including the National Food Security Nutrition Mission (NFSNM), National Mission on Edible Oils–Oilseeds (NMEO-Oilseeds), Mission for Aatmanirbharta in Pulses, RashtriyaKrishiVikasYojana (RKVY), National Mission on High Yielding Seeds (NMHYS) and Mission for Cotton Productivity. These initiatives also support research and innovation in modern crop improvement technologies, including genome editing, molecular breeding, biofortification, agrobiodiversity and establishment of a Global R&D Hub for Millets.

To address the challenges posed by climate change and strengthen national food security, the Government promotes the development and adoption of high-yielding, climate-resilient and bio-fortified crop varieties through the National Agricultural Research and Education System (NARES). ICAR Institutes and the private sector are using advanced crop improvement technologies such as Marker-Assisted Selection (MAS), Gene Editing and Speed Breeding to accelerate the development of improved crop varieties.

The Government also promotes production and dissemination of these varieties through the breeder, foundation and certified seed chain and collaborates with international agricultural research institutions of the CGIAR system for exchange of knowledge, germplasm, best practices and climate-resilient seed technologies. During the period from 2014 to 2025, 2,996 climate-resilient crop varieties have been developed by the National Agricultural Research and Education System (NARES), thereby strengthening the resilience of Indian agriculture to climate change while contributing to enhanced agricultural productivity and national food security.

This information was given by Minister of State for Agriculture and Farmers Welfare, Shri Ramnath Thakur in a written reply in Lok Sabha today.

(Release ID: 2287250)

headline: Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana
fullText:
Ministry of Agriculture &

Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana

21 JUL 2026 6:03PM by PIB Delhi

Pradhan Manti KrishiSinchayeeYojana (PMKSY) is an umbrella scheme, consisting of three components - Accelerated Irrigation Benefit Programme (AIBP) and HarKhetKoPani (HKKP)being implemented by the Department of Water Resources, River Development & Ganga Rejuvenation (DoWR,RD&GR), Ministry of Jal Shakti, and Watershed Development Component (WDC) being implemented by Department of Land Resources, Ministry of Rural Development.Central Assistance is provided to the States under PMKSY for creation of irrigation potential, command area development and on farm development activities. Projects under the Scheme are generally implemented by State Governments through competitive tendering processes, in which private contractors and enterprises may participate in accordance with the applicable procurement rules of the respective State Governments. The works under the scheme are executed as per State norms. The scheme does not contain any specific provision for private sector investments.

Capacity building and training activities relating to efficient water management are undertaken by the implementing States/Union Territories under the relevant components of PMKSY, as per scheme guidelines. However, consolidated State-wise data on the number of farmers and field-level workers trained is not maintained centrally.

This information was given by Minister of State for Agriculture and Farmers Welfare, Shri Ramnath Thakur in a written reply in Lok Sabha today.

(Release ID: 2287248)

headline: Selection Methodology under Prime Minister Dhan-DhaanyaKrishiYojana
fullText:
Ministry of Agriculture &

Selection Methodology under Prime Minister Dhan-DhaanyaKrishiYojana

21 JUL 2026 6:03PM by PIB Delhi

The 100 districts under Prime Minister Dhan-DhaanyaKrishiYojana (DDKY) have been selected based on three indicators of low productivity, low cropping intensity and less agriculture credit disbursement.

The number of districts for each State is based on net cropped area and number of operational holdings, ensuring balanced regional development and fostering agricultural growth where it is most needed.

The methodology used published data, which is already in public domain, of above mentioned three indicators. Each State was covered under DDKY, with at least 1 district, based on net cropped area and number of operational holdings, hence question of exclusion does not arise. Further, if States identify additional districts that require similar focused attention, they may adopt and replicate the same framework and approach in those districts using their existing administrative mechanisms and available scheme resources.

This information was given by Minister of State for Agriculture and Farmers Welfare, Shri Ramnath Thakur in a written reply in Lok Sabha today.

(Release ID: 2287249)
